Title: Prospective randomized comparison of anatomic single- and double-bundle anterior cruciate ligament reconstruction.
abstract:
Purpose  
To compare anatomic double-bundle anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) reconstruction with anatomic single-bundle reconstruction in terms of knee joint stability and function.

Methods  
A prospective randomized clinical study was conducted with 32 cases of anatomic single-bundle ACL reconstruction and 34 cases of anatomic double-bundle ACL reconstruction, with an average follow-up of 16.3 ± 3.1 months. Tunnel placements were measured using 3D CT. Clinical outcomes were assessed post-reconstruction.

Results  
Tunnel placements were confirmed to be in anatomic positions in both the single-bundle (SB) and double-bundle (DB) groups. No significant differences were observed between the SB and DB groups in clinical outcome scores, pivot shift test, and KT 1000 measurements (average side-to-side difference for anterior tibial translation was 0.7 mm in the SB group and 1.0 mm in the DB group). In the DB group, 44.1% of PL bundle grafts were rated as excellent, while 11.8% were in poor condition. No new meniscal tears were observed in either group. However, cartilage damage in the medial patellofemoral joint occurred in 38.2% of cases in the DB group, compared to 9.3% in the SB group.

Conclusion  
The treatment with anatomic double-bundle ACL reconstruction was not more effective than anatomic single-bundle reconstruction in patients with knee joint stability and function as the primary outcome.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your knee is like a hinge on a door.  Doctors sometimes need to fix a torn ligament (a strong band of tissue) in the knee, called the ACL.  This study looked at two ways to fix it:  a single-bundle fix (like fixing one hinge) and a double-bundle fix (like fixing two hinges).

They followed 32 kids who got the single-bundle fix and 34 kids who got the double-bundle fix for a while.  They checked how well the fixes worked by measuring things like how stable the knee was and how well it moved.

The results showed that both fixes worked pretty much the same.  The double-bundle fix had a few more problems, like some of the extra parts of the fix weren't working as well as expected, and there was more damage to the cartilage in the knee.

So, the study found that fixing the ACL with one bundle was just as good as fixing it with two bundles, at least in terms of how stable and functional the knee became.

Title: Prospective randomized comparison of anatomic single- and double-bundle anterior cruciate ligament reconstruction.
abstract:
Purpose
To determine if anatomic double-bundle anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) reconstruction is superior to anatomic single-bundle reconstruction in restoring the stabilities and functions of the knee joint. 
Methods
A prospective randomized clinical study was done to compare the results of 32 cases of anatomic single-bundle ACL reconstruction and 34 cases of anatomic double-bundle ACL reconstruction with average follow-up of 16.3 В± 3.1В months. Tunnel placements of all the cases were measured on 3D CT. Clinical results were collected after reconstruction; graft's appearance, meniscus status and cartilage state under arthroscopy were compared and analysed too. 
Results
Tunnel placements, confirmed with 3D CT, were in the anatomic positions as described in literature both in SB and DB group. No differences were found between SB and DB groups in clinical outcome scores, pivot shift test and KT 1000В measurements (average side-to-side difference for anterior tibial translation was 0.7В mm in SB group and 1.0В mm in DB group). More than 70 % of the single-bundle graft and AM bundle graft in DB group appeared excellent, but only 44.1 % of PL bundle grafts in DB group were excellent and 11.8 % were in poor state. No new menisci tear was found either in SB or DB group, however, in DB group cartilage damages in medial patella-femoral joint occurred in 38.2 % cases. This rate was significantly higher than in the SB group which is only 9.3 %. 
Conclusion
Both single- and double-bundle anatomic ACL reconstruction can restore the knee's stability and functions very well. However, more incidences of poor PL status and medial patellar-femoral cartilage damage may occur in double-bundle ACL reconstruction.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your knee is like a really complicated machine.  Doctors sometimes need to fix the ACL (a strong band inside the knee) if it's torn.  This study looked at two different ways to fix it:  a single-bundle fix (like fixing one important part) and a double-bundle fix (like fixing two important parts).

They followed 32 kids who got the single-bundle fix and 34 who got the double-bundle fix.  They checked how well the fixes worked by looking at things like how straight the knee was, how much it moved, and the health of the cartilage and other parts of the knee.

The results showed that both fixes worked pretty well in making the knee stable and strong again.  But the double-bundle fix had a few more problems.  Some of the extra parts of the double-bundle fix didn't heal as well, and there was a higher chance of some cartilage damage in the knee.

So, both fixes are good, but the single-bundle fix might be a little better because it has fewer problems.

Title: Intravenous dextrose for children with gastroenteritis and dehydration: a double-blind randomized controlled trial.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim benefit based on (+) secondary endpoint
abstract:
STUDY OBJECTIVE: We seek to determine whether an initial intravenous bolus of 5% dextrose in normal saline solution compared with normal saline solution will lead to a lower proportion of hospitalized patients and a greater reduction in serum ketone levels in children with gastroenteritis and dehydration.

METHODS: We enrolled children aged 6 months to 6 years in a double-blind, randomized controlled trial of patients presenting to a pediatric emergency department. Subjects were randomized to receive a 20 mL/kg infusion of either 5% dextrose in normal saline solution or normal saline solution. Serum ketone levels were measured before and at 1- and 2-hour intervals after the initial study fluid bolus administration. Primary outcome was the proportion of children hospitalized. Secondary outcome was change in serum ketone levels over time.

RESULTS: One hundred eighty-eight children were enrolled. The proportion of children hospitalized did not differ between groups (35% in the 5% dextrose in normal saline solution group versus 44% in the normal saline solution group; risk difference 9%; 95% confidence interval [CI] -5% to 22%). Compared with children who received normal saline solution, those who received 5% dextrose in normal saline solution had a greater reduction in mean serum ketone levels at both 1 hour (mean О” 1.2 versus 0.1 mmol/L; mean difference 1.1 mmol/L; 95% CI 0.4 to 1.9 mmol/L) and 2 hours (mean О” 1.9 versus 0.3 mmol/L; mean difference 1.6 mmol/L; 95% CI 0.9 to 2.3 mmol/L).

CONCLUSION: Administration of a dextrose-containing bolus compared with normal saline did not lead to a lower rate of hospitalization for children with gastroenteritis and dehydration. There was, however, a greater reduction in serum ketone levels in patients who received 5% dextrose in normal saline solution.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine you're sick with a tummy bug and are dehydrated.  Doctors gave some kids a special liquid with sugar (dextrose) and water (normal saline), and other kids just plain water (normal saline).  They wanted to see if the sugar liquid would help the kids get better faster and lower their "ketone" levels (a sign of sickness).

They followed a bunch of kids (188) who came to the hospital.  They split them into two groups, and neither the kids nor the doctors knew which liquid each group got.  They measured how sick the kids were (how many needed to stay in the hospital) and how their ketone levels changed over two hours.

The results?  Giving the sugar liquid didn't make a difference in how many kids needed to stay in the hospital.  But the kids who got the sugar liquid had much lower ketone levels after one and two hours compared to the kids who got plain water.  So, the sugar liquid helped lower the sickness markers, but it didn't help them get out of the hospital any faster.

plain_language_summary:
Doctors wanted to see if giving kids with tummy bugs and dehydration a special liquid (5% dextrose in normal saline) would keep them from needing to stay in the hospital more often than just giving them regular saline.

They did a test with lots of kids (188) who came to the hospital.  They randomly put the kids into two groups. One group got the special liquid, and the other got regular saline.  They didn't tell the kids or the doctors which liquid each kid got.

The results showed that the special liquid didn't help keep kids from needing to stay in the hospital any better than the regular liquid.  About 35% of the kids who got the special liquid ended up needing to stay in the hospital, and about 44% of the kids who got regular liquid needed to stay.  So, the special liquid didn't make a big difference.

Title: A randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial of a highly purified equine F(ab)2 antibody black widow spider antivenom.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Focus on another objective (e.g., trial is (-) but they say they accomplish some goal that they did not prespecify)
abstract:
STUDY OBJECTIVE: Black widow spider antivenom has never been tested in a randomized clinical trial, to our knowledge. We evaluate the efficacy of a novel F(ab)2 antivenom in patients with moderate to severe pain caused by black widow spider envenomation.

METHODS: A randomized, placebo-controlled, double-blind clinical trial was conducted in 12 academic emergency departments. We included patients at least 10 years old with moderate to severe latrodectism. Subjects received either a single intravenous infusion of antivenom or placebo. Pain was assessed with the visual analog scale. The primary efficacy outcome was the difference in pre- and posttreatment visual analog scale score.

RESULTS: Twenty-four subjects were enrolled between October 2005 and October 2006; 13 were randomized to antivenom and 11 to placebo. The median change in visual analog scale at 150 minutes posttreatment was -50.0 mm (Interquartile Range [IQR] -67, -41 mm) in the antivenom treatment group and -46.0 mm (IQR -51, 0 mm) in the placebo treatment group (P=.14). No serious adverse events or deaths were reported.

CONCLUSION: The treatment with antivenom was not more effective than placebo in patients with moderate to severe pain caused by black widow spider envenomation.
plain_language_summary:
Scientists wanted to see if a new medicine (antivenom) helps people who are bitten by black widow spiders.  They didn't know if this medicine worked because it hadn't been tested before.

They did a special test with 24 people who were bitten.  Half of the people got the new antivenom, and half got a fake medicine (placebo).  Neither the people nor the doctors knew who got which medicine.  They measured how much pain the people felt before and after getting the medicine using a pain scale.

After the test, the scientists found that the antivenom didn't make the pain go away any better than the fake medicine.  Everyone was okay, and no one died.

So, the scientists learned that this new antivenom doesn't seem to work better than a fake medicine for black widow spider bites.

Title: A randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial of a highly purified equine F(ab)2 antibody black widow spider antivenom.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Focus on another objective (e.g., trial is (-) but they say they accomplish some goal that they did not prespecify)
abstract:
STUDY OBJECTIVE: Black widow spider antivenom has never been tested in a randomized clinical trial, to our knowledge. We explore various efficacy measures for a novel F(ab)2 antivenom in patients with moderate to severe pain caused by black widow spider envenomation.

METHODS: A randomized, placebo-controlled, double-blind, clinical trial was conducted in 12 academic emergency departments. We included patients at least 10 years old with moderate to severe latrodectism. Subjects received either a single intravenous infusion of antivenom or placebo. Pain was assessed with the visual analog scale. The primary efficacy outcome was the difference in pre- and posttreatment visual analog scale score. Prospectively defined secondary outcomes included treatment failures and time to clinically important decrease in pain.

RESULTS: Twenty-four subjects were enrolled between October 2005 and October 2006; 13 were randomized to antivenom and 11 to placebo. The median change in visual analog scale at 150 minutes posttreatment was -50.0 mm (Interquartile Range [IQR] -67, -41 mm) in the antivenom treatment group and -46.0 mm (IQR -51, 0 mm) in the placebo treatment group (P=.14). There were 7 treatment failures (64%; 95% confidence interval 35% to 92%) in the placebo group and 3 (23%; 95% confidence interval 0.2% to 46%) in the antivenom group (P=.06). The median time to a clinically important decrease in pain after treatment was shorter in the antivenom group compared with the placebo group (30 minutes [IQR 30, 60 minutes] versus 90 minutes [IQR 30, 90 minutes]; P=.03). No serious adverse events or deaths were reported.

CONCLUSION: Although the overall reduction in pain was similar for antivenom- and placebo-treated subjects, antivenom reduced pain more rapidly than placebo. No significant adverse events occurred in either group.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine you have a really bad spider bite.  Doctors wanted to see if a special medicine (antivenom) could help.  They didn't know if this medicine was really good, because no one had ever tested it this way before.

So, they did a special test with 24 people who had bad spider bites.  They randomly put some people in a group that got the medicine, and others in a group that got a fake medicine (placebo).  Both groups didn't know which medicine they got.

They measured how much pain the people felt before and after getting the medicine using a scale.  They also looked at how long it took for the pain to go away.

The results showed that the medicine helped reduce pain, but it didn't do it much faster than the fake medicine.  The medicine did seem to make the pain go away faster, though.  There were a few people in each group who didn't get better, but the medicine group had fewer people who didn't get better.  No one had any serious problems from the medicine.

In short, the medicine helped, but it didn't help *significantly* better than the fake medicine.  It did seem to make the pain go away faster.

Title: Efficacy of direct arthroscopy-guided suprascapular nerve block after arthroscopic rotator cuff repair: a prospective randomized study.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim benefit based on (+) secondary endpoint
abstract:
Purpose: The purpose of this study was to evaluate the outcomes of arthroscopy-guided direct suprascapular nerve block performed after arthroscopic rotator cuff repair.

Methods: In this prospective, randomized, double-blinded clinical study, 30 patients were divided into two groups: 15 patients (group I) were treated with arthroscopy-guided suprascapular nerve block using 10 mL 0.5% ropivacaine with 1:200,000 epinephrine, and 15 patients (group II) were treated with placebo using 10 mL 0.9% saline after arthroscopic rotator cuff repair. The primary outcome was patient pain levels measured using the visual analog scale (VAS) at 1, 3, 6, 12, 18, and 24 hours post-operatively. The number of boluses and total amount of fentanyl dispensed by patient-controlled analgesia administration during the 24-hour post-operative period were also evaluated.

Results: VAS scores did not differ significantly between groups I and II during the 24-hour post-operative period. Mean fentanyl bolus consumption was less in group I compared with group II (p = 0.015).

Conclusion: The treatment with arthroscopy-guided suprascapular nerve block was not more effective than placebo in reducing post-operative pain in patients with arthroscopic rotator cuff repair.
plain_language_summary:
Doctors did a study to see if a special injection could help people who had their rotator cuff (a group of muscles in their shoulder) fixed with surgery.  

They divided the people into two groups.  One group got an injection that numbed a nerve near the shoulder, and the other group got a fake injection (a salt water solution).  They measured how much pain the people felt for 24 hours after surgery, and how much pain medicine they needed.

The injection that numbed the nerve didn't make a big difference in how much pain the people felt.  But the people who got the nerve-numbing injection needed less pain medicine.

So, the study showed that the nerve-numbing injection wasn't better than a fake injection at reducing pain after rotator cuff surgery.

Title: Efficacy of direct arthroscopy-guided suprascapular nerve block after arthroscopic rotator cuff repair: a prospective randomized study.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim benefit based on (+) secondary endpoint
abstract:
Purpose: The purpose of this study was to evaluate the outcomes of arthroscopy-guided direct suprascapular nerve block performed after arthroscopic rotator cuff repair.

Methods: In the present prospective, randomized, double-blinded clinical study, 30 patients were divided into two groups: 15 patients (group I) were treated with arthroscopy-guided suprascapular nerve block using 10 mL 0.5 % ropivacaine with 1:200,000 epinephrine, and 15 patients (group II) were treated with placebo using 10 mL 0.9 % saline after arthroscopic rotator cuff repair. Patient pain levels were measured using the visual analog scale (VAS) at 1, 3, 6, 12, 18, and 24 h post-operatively. Additionally, the number of boluses and total amount of fentanyl dispensed by patient-controlled analgesia administration during the 24-h post-operative period were evaluated.

Results: VAS scores did not differ significantly between groups I and II during the 24-h post-operative period, but mean fentanyl bolus consumption was significantly less in group I compared with group II (p = 0.015).

Conclusion: Arthroscopy-guided suprascapular nerve block at the end of a rotator cuff repair was safe and less time-consuming than expected. Although this procedure did not significantly reduce the post-operative pain, the post-operative need for fentanyl boluses as analgesia was reduced significantly, and it would be beneficial if this procedure involved a sensory branch of axillary nerve block or was performed at the beginning of the arthroscopic procedure.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your arm is hurt, and doctors need to fix it.  They're doing a surgery called a rotator cuff repair.

This study looked at a special way to numb the area around the shoulder during and after the surgery.  They used a tiny camera (arthroscopy) to guide a needle to a nerve in the shoulder, and injected medicine to block pain signals.  Some patients got real medicine, and some got a fake medicine (placebo).

They measured how much pain the patients felt using a scale (like a thermometer for pain).  They also tracked how much pain medicine (fentanyl) the patients needed.

The study found that the special nerve block didn't make the pain go away completely, but it did help patients need less pain medicine.  It was also faster than expected.

The researchers think this method is good, but they might make it even better by adding another nerve block or doing it earlier in the surgery.

Title: Does providing prescription information or services improve medication adherence among patients discharged from the emergency department? A randomized controlled trial.
abstract:
Study Objective: We determine whether prescription information or services improve the medication adherence of emergency department (ED) patients.

Methods: Adult patients treated at one of 3 EDs between November 2010 and September 2011 and prescribed an antibiotic, central nervous system, gastrointestinal, cardiac, or respiratory drug at discharge were eligible. Subjects were randomly assigned to usual care or one of 3 prescription information or services intervention groups: (1) practical services to reduce barriers to prescription filling; (2) consumer drug information from MedlinePlus; or (3) both services and information. Self-reported medication adherence, measured by primary adherence (prescription filling) and persistence (receiving medicine as prescribed) rates, was determined during a telephone interview 1 week postdischarge.

Results: Of the 3,940 subjects enrolled and randomly allocated to treatment, 86% (N=3,386) completed the follow-up interview. Overall, primary adherence was 88% and persistence was 48%. Primary adherence and persistence did not differ significantly between usual care and the prescription information or services groups.

Conclusion: The treatment was not more effective than comparator in patients with medication adherence in the emergency department setting.
plain_language_summary:
Doctors wanted to see if giving patients extra help with their medicine after leaving the emergency room (ER) would make them more likely to take their medicine as prescribed.

They looked at 3,940 grown-ups who got medicine in the ER.  Some got no extra help, some got help filling their prescriptions, some got information about their medicine from a website, and some got both.

One week later, the doctors called the patients to ask if they took their medicine.  Most of the patients (86%) talked to the doctors.

They found that even with the extra help, most people still didn't take all their medicine as the doctor said.  About 88% of people filled their prescriptions, but only about 48% took all their medicine as prescribed.  The extra help didn't seem to make a big difference.

So, the extra help with medicine after the ER visit didn't really help people take their medicine better.

Title: Does providing prescription information or services improve medication adherence among patients discharged from the emergency department? A randomized controlled trial.
abstract:
Study objective: We determine whether prescription information or services improve the medication adherence of emergency department (ED) patients.

Methods: Adult patients treated at one of 3 EDs between November 2010 and September 2011 and prescribed an antibiotic, central nervous system, gastrointestinal, cardiac, or respiratory drug at discharge were eligible. Subjects were randomly assigned to usual care or one of 3 prescription information or services intervention groups: (1) practical services to reduce barriers to prescription filling (practical prescription information or services); (2) consumer drug information from MedlinePlus (MedlinePlus prescription information or services); or (3) both services and information (combination prescription information or services). Self-reported medication adherence, measured by primary adherence (prescription filling) and persistence (receiving medicine as prescribed) rates, was determined during a telephone interview 1 week postdischarge.

Results: Of the 3,940 subjects enrolled and randomly allocated to treatment, 86% (N=3,386) completed the follow-up interview. Overall, primary adherence was 88% and persistence was 48%. Across the sites, primary adherence and persistence did not differ significantly between usual care and the prescription information or services groups. However, at site C, subjects who received the practical prescription information or services (odds ratio [OR]=2.4; 95% confidence interval [CI] 1.4 to 4.3) or combination prescription information or services (OR=1.8; 95% CI 1.1 to 3.1) were more likely to fill their prescription compared with usual care. Among subjects prescribed a drug that treats an underlying condition, subjects who received the practical prescription information or services were more likely to fill their prescription (OR=1.8; 95% CI 1.0 to 3.1) compared with subjects who received usual care.

Conclusion: Prescription filling and receiving medications as prescribed was not meaningfully improved by offering patients patient-centered prescription information and services.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine a doctor gives you medicine after you're sick at the hospital.  This study looked at how well people actually take that medicine as directed.

They picked a bunch of people who got medicine at the hospital and split them into groups.  Some people got normal care.  Other groups got extra help with their medicine, like getting clear instructions on how to take it or help getting it filled.

The researchers called everyone a week later to see if they took their medicine.  They looked at how many people filled their prescription (that's called "primary adherence") and how many people took the medicine as the doctor said (that's called "persistence").

Overall, most people filled their prescriptions, but not everyone took their medicine as directed.  The extra help with the medicine didn't really help most people take their medicine better overall.  But at one hospital, the extra help with getting the medicine filled *did* help some people more than the usual care.  It helped even more if they got both kinds of help.  The extra help also helped people who were taking medicine for a longer-term condition.

So, the study found that extra help with medicine instructions and getting it filled didn't really help everyone take their medicine better, but it did help some people at one hospital.

Title: Patient-specific guides do not improve accuracy in total knee arthroplasty: a prospective randomized controlled trial.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim benefit based only on efficacy, ignore safety (or vice versa)
abstract:
Background: Patient-specific guides (PSGs) have been introduced for total knee arthroplasty (TKA) to potentially improve component positioning accuracy. However, this has not been confirmed in a controlled prospective trial.

Methods: In this randomized controlled trial, we enrolled 128 patients to compare three-planar component alignment and overall coronal mechanical alignment between PSG and conventional instrumentation. The primary outcome was the number of outliers in overall coronal alignment. All patients underwent long-leg standing radiography and CT scan. Deviation of more than 3° from the target in any plane was defined as an outlier.

Results: The PSG and conventional cohorts showed similar numbers of outliers in overall coronal alignment (25% versus 28%; p = 0.69). There were more outliers in tibial coronal (15% versus 3%; p = 0.03) and sagittal (21% versus 3%; p = 0.002) alignment in the PSG group than in the conventional group. PSGs were abandoned in 14 patients (22%) and modified in 18 (28%).

Conclusions: The treatment with PSG was not more effective than conventional instrumentation in patients with TKA regarding the primary outcome of overall coronal alignment.
plain_language_summary:
Doctors use special tools to put parts in place when fixing a person's knee (total knee replacement).  Some new tools (called patient-specific guides) are supposed to make the parts go in more precisely.

This study looked at whether these new tools were better than the old ones.  They put the new tools on one group of people and the old tools on another group.  They took pictures and scans of the knees to see how well the parts were placed.

The study found that the new tools didn't make the parts go in any better overall.  But, the new tools did sometimes put the parts in the wrong place in some ways (like the front-back or side-to-side parts of the knee).  In fact, the new tools were abandoned or changed in a lot of people.

So, the new tools aren't better than the old ones for putting the knee parts in the right place.

Title: Patient-specific guides do not improve accuracy in total knee arthroplasty: a prospective randomized controlled trial.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim benefit based only on efficacy, ignore safety (or vice versa)
abstract:
Background: Recently, patient-specific guides (PSGs) have been introduced, claiming a significant improvement in accuracy and reproducibility of component positioning in TKA. Despite intensive marketing by the manufacturers, this claim has not yet been confirmed in a controlled prospective trial.

Questions/purposes: We (1) compared three-planar component alignment and overall coronal mechanical alignment between PSG and conventional instrumentation and (2) logged the need for applying changes in the suggested position of the PSG.

Methods: In this randomized controlled trial, we enrolled 128 patients. In the PSG cohort, surgical navigation was used as an intraoperative control. When the suggested cut deviated more than 3Ў from target, the use of PSG was abandoned and marked as an outlier. When cranial-caudal position or size was adapted, the PSG was marked as modified. All patients underwent long-leg standing radiography and CT scan. Deviation of more than 3Ў from the target in any plane was defined as an outlier.

Results: The PSG and conventional cohorts showed similar numbers of outliers in overall coronal alignment (25% versus 28%; p = 0.69), femoral coronal alignment (7% versus 14%) (p = 0.24), and femoral axial alignment (23% versus 17%; p = 0.50). There were more outliers in tibial coronal (15% versus 3%; p = 0.03) and sagittal 21% versus 3%; p = 0.002) alignment in the PSG group than in the conventional group. PSGs were abandoned in 14 patients (22%) and modified in 18 (28%).

Conclusions: PSGs do not improve accuracy in TKA and, in our experience, were somewhat impractical in that the procedure needed to be either modified or abandoned with some frequency.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine doctors are trying out a new tool to help them put knee replacements in the right place.  This tool is called a "patient-specific guide" (PSG).  The makers say it's much better than the old way, but no one had really tested it.

So, doctors did a test with 128 people getting knee replacements.  Half got the new tool (PSG), and half got the old way.  They carefully measured how well the knee replacements were placed.

The results?  The new tool wasn't better at putting the knee replacement in the right place overall.  In some ways, it was even worse.  The new tool sometimes gave the wrong measurements, and doctors had to change it or stop using it quite often.  So, the new tool wasn't as helpful as the old one.

Title: N-acetylcysteine plus intravenous fluids versus intravenous fluids alone to prevent contrast-induced nephropathy in emergency computed tomography.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Focus on another objective (e.g., trial is (-) but they say they accomplish some goal that they did not prespecify)
abstract:
STUDY OBJECTIVE: We test the hypothesis that N-acetylcysteine plus normal saline solution is more effective than normal saline solution alone in the prevention of contrast-induced nephropathy.

METHODS: The design was a randomized, double blind, 2-center, placebo-controlled interventional trial. Inclusion criteria were patients undergoing chest, abdominal, or pelvic computed tomography (CT) scan with intravenous contrast, older than 18 years, and at least one contrast-induced nephropathy risk factor. Exclusion criteria were end-stage renal disease, pregnancy, N-acetylcysteine allergy, or clinical instability. Intervention for the treatment group was N-acetylcysteine 3 g in 500 mL normal saline solution as an intravenous bolus and then 200 mg/hour (67 mL/hour) for up to 24 hours; and for the placebo group was 500 mL normal saline solution and then 67 mL/hour for up to 24 hours. The primary outcome was contrast-induced nephropathy, defined as an increase in creatinine level of 25% or 0.5 mg/dL, measured 48 to 72 hours after CT.

RESULTS: The data safety and monitoring board terminated the study early for futility. Of 399 patients enrolled, 357 (89%) completed follow-up and were included. The N-acetylcysteine plus saline solution group contrast-induced nephropathy rate was 14 of 185 (7.6%) versus 12 of 172 (7.0%) in the normal saline solution only group (absolute risk difference 0.6%; 95% confidence interval -4.8% to 6.0%). The contrast-induced nephropathy rate in patients receiving less than 1 L intravenous fluids in the emergency department (ED) was 19 of 147 (12.9%) versus 7 of 210 (3.3%) for greater than 1 L intravenous fluids (difference 9.6%; 95% confidence interval 3.7% to 15.5%), a 69% risk reduction (odds ratio 0.41; 95% confidence interval 0.21 to 0.80) per liter of intravenous fluids.

CONCLUSION: We did not find evidence of a benefit for N-acetylcysteine administration to our ED patients undergoing contrast-enhanced CT. However, we did find a significant association between volume of intravenous fluids administered and reduction in contrast-induced nephropathy.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine you're getting a special X-ray called a CT scan.  Sometimes, this scan needs a special liquid (contrast) to make the pictures clearer.  But this liquid can sometimes hurt your kidneys.  

This study looked at a way to protect the kidneys.  They gave some people a medicine called N-acetylcysteine with the liquid, and others just the liquid.  They wanted to see if the medicine helped prevent kidney problems after the CT scan.

They followed a lot of people who got the CT scan.  They measured how their kidneys were doing after the scan.  The results showed that the medicine didn't really help prevent kidney problems.  But, they did find that giving more liquid to people before and after the scan helped a lot to protect their kidneys.  So, getting enough liquid is important for protecting your kidneys when you have a CT scan with contrast.

Inclusion criteria were patients undergoing chest, abdominal, or pelvic computed tomography (CT) scan with intravenous contrast, older than 18 years, and at least one contrast-induced nephropathy risk factor. Exclusion criteria were end-stage renal disease, pregnancy, N-acetylcysteine allergy, or clinical instability. Intervention for the treatment group was N-acetylcysteine 3 g in 500 mL normal saline solution as an intravenous bolus and then 200 mg/hour (67 mL/hour) for up to 24 hours; and for the placebo group was 500 mL normal saline solution and then 67 mL/hour for up to 24 hours. The primary outcome was contrast-induced nephropathy, defined as an increase in creatinine level of 25% or 0.5 mg/dL, measured 48 to 72 hours after CT.

RESULTS: The data safety and monitoring board terminated the study early for futility. Of 399 patients enrolled, 357 (89%) completed follow-up and were included. The N-acetylcysteine plus saline solution group contrast-induced nephropathy rate was 14 of 185 (7.6%) versus 12 of 172 (7.0%) in the normal saline solution only group (absolute risk difference 0.6%; 95% confidence interval -4.8% to 6.0%).

CONCLUSION: The treatment with N-acetylcysteine was not more effective than normal saline solution in patients undergoing contrast-enhanced CT in preventing contrast-induced nephropathy.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine you're getting a special X-ray called a CT scan.  Sometimes, this X-ray needs a special liquid to make the pictures clearer.  This liquid can sometimes hurt your kidneys.  

This study looked at whether a medicine called N-acetylcysteine could help prevent this kidney problem.  They gave some people the medicine with the liquid, and others just the liquid.  They carefully watched both groups to see who got the kidney problem.

The study found that the medicine didn't really help more than just the liquid.  Both groups had about the same number of people who got the kidney problem. So, the medicine didn't make a big difference.

Title: Effect of video laryngoscopy on trauma patient survival: a randomized controlled trial.
Journal: Journal of Trauma and Acute Care Surgery
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim benefit based on (+) subgroup analysis
abstract:
Background: Many resuscitation scenarios include the use of emergency intubation to support injured patients. New video-guided airway management technology is available, which may minimize the risk to patients from this procedure.

Methods: This was a controlled clinical trial conducted in the trauma receiving unit in a university-affiliated urban hospital in which 623 consecutive adult patients requiring emergency airway management were prospectively randomized to intubation with either the direct laryngoscope (DL) or the GlideScope video laryngoscope (GVL) device. The primary outcome was survival to hospital discharge.

Results: There was no significant difference in mortality between the GVL group (28 [9%] of 303) and the DL group (24 [8%] of 320) (p = 0.43) for all patients. Among all patients, median intubation duration in seconds was higher for the GVL group (median, 56; interquartile range, 40-81) than for the DL group (median, 40; interquartile range, 24-68) (p < 0.001). There were no significant differences between the two groups in first-pass success (80% for GVL and 81% for DL, p = 0.46).

Conclusion: The GlideScope was not more effective than direct laryngoscopy in patients with respect to survival to hospital discharge.
plain_language_summary:
Doctors sometimes have to put a tube down a person's throat to help them breathe.  This study looked at two different ways to do this quickly during an emergency.

One way is the old-fashioned way, using a special tool called a direct laryngoscope (DL).  The other way is a newer, video-guided way using a GlideScope (GVL).

They tested both methods on a lot of people who needed emergency breathing help in a hospital.  They randomly chose which method to use for each person.

The study found that both methods worked about the same.  There wasn't a big difference in how many people lived.  The GVL method took a little longer to put the tube in, but the success rate was about the same.

So, the new video-guided method wasn't better than the older method for helping people survive.

Title: Effect of video laryngoscopy on trauma patient survival: a randomized controlled trial.
Journal: Journal of Trauma and Acute Care Surgery

abstract:
Background: Many resuscitation scenarios include the use of emergency intubation to support injured patients. New video-guided airway management technology is available, which may minimize the risk to patients from this procedure.

Methods: This was a controlled clinical trial conducted in the trauma receiving unit in a university-affiliated urban hospital in which 623 consecutive adult patients requiring emergency airway management were prospectively randomized to intubation with either the direct laryngoscope (DL) or the GlideScope video laryngoscope (GVL) device.

Among all patients, median intubation duration in seconds was significantly higher for the GVL group (median, 56; interquartile range, 40-81) than for the DL group (median, 40; interquartile range, 24-68) (p < 0.001). Correspondingly, this group also experienced a greater incidence of low oxygen saturations of 80% or less (27 [50%] of 54 for the GVL group and 15 [24%] of 63 for the DL group; p = 0.004). There were no significant differences between the two groups in first-pass success (80% for GVL and 81% for DL, p = 0.46).

Conclusion: Use of the GlideScope did not influence survival to hospital discharge among all patients and was associated with longer intubation times than direct laryngoscopy. Among the video laryngoscope cohort, a smaller subgroup of severe head injury trauma patients identified retrospectively seemed to be associated with a greater incidence of hypoxia of 80% or less and mortality.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine doctors have two ways to put a tube down a person's throat to help them breathe after an accident.  One is the old way (direct laryngoscope) and the other is a new way (GlideScope).  

Researchers tested both methods on a bunch of people who needed this breathing tube after injuries.  They looked at how many people lived and how long it took to put the tube in.

Overall, both methods worked about the same.  The same number of people lived, no matter which method was used.

But, for people with very serious head injuries, the new GlideScope method seemed to be worse.  More of these people died using the GlideScope, and it took longer to put the tube in, which meant their oxygen levels got dangerously low more often.

So, the new GlideScope method is not better for everyone, but it might be worse for people with very serious head injuries.  Doctors need to think carefully about which method to use depending on the patient's injuries.

Title: Tenotomy or tenodesis for long head biceps lesions in shoulders with reparable rotator cuff tears: a prospective randomised trial.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim benefit based on (+) secondary endpoint
abstract:
Purpose: The aim of this study was to compare the clinical outcomes of tenotomy and tenodesis in patients older than 55 years of age affected by reparable rotator cuff tears with concomitant long head biceps pathologies.

Methods: Patients older than 55 years of age with long head biceps lesions and reparable rotator cuff tears were selected for this study. A total of 151 patients were randomly assigned to the tenotomy group (77 patients) or the tenodesis group (74 patients). Arthroscopic rotator cuff repair was performed in all the patients. Before surgery, physical and radiological examinations were performed; the constant score was measured as well. After the operation, the surgical time, cost, pain (VAS scores), Popeye sign, cramping pain, constant scores, satisfaction level, and the elbow flexion and forearm supination strength indices were recorded.

Results: Patients were followed for an average of 24 months. No significant differences in the clinical results for the constant scores, the forearm supination and elbow flexion strength indices, Popeye sign, cramping pain, and satisfaction level were found between the groups. Tenotomy required a shorter surgical time (40.4 ± 4.0 vs. 50.4 ± 5.9 min, P < 0.001) and resulted in faster pain relief (3.1 ± 1.8 vs. 4.8 ± 1.9, P < 0.001).

Conclusion: The treatment tenotomy was not more effective than tenodesis in patients with long head biceps lesions and reparable rotator cuff tears.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your arm is a machine with a broken part (the rotator cuff).  Doctors tried two different ways to fix it:  one way (tenotomy) cut a tendon, and the other (tenodesis) moved and fixed a tendon.  They did this on 151 older people who also had a problem with another tendon (the long head biceps).

They followed these people for about two years to see how well each fix worked.  They looked at things like how much pain the people felt, how strong their arms were, and how happy they were with the results.

The results showed that both ways worked about the same, in terms of arm strength, pain, and happiness.  However, cutting the tendon (tenotomy) took less time and made the pain go away faster.

So, the study showed that cutting the tendon was just as good as moving and fixing it, but it was quicker and helped with pain relief faster.

Title: Tenotomy or tenodesis for long head biceps lesions in shoulders with reparable rotator cuff tears: a prospective randomised trial.

abstract:
Purpose: Tenotomy and tenodesis are both effective for the treatment of long head biceps lesions. The aim of this study was to compare the clinical outcomes of the two procedures in patients older than 55 years of age affected by reparable rotator cuff tears with concomitant long head biceps pathologies.

Methods: Patients older than 55 years of age with long head biceps lesions and reparable rotator cuff tears were selected for this study. A total of 151 patients were randomly assigned to the tenotomy group (77 patients) or the tenodesis group (74 patients). Arthroscopic rotator cuff repair was performed in all the patients. Results: Patients were followed for an average of 24 months. No significant differences in the clinical results for the constant scores, the forearm supination and elbow flexion strength indices, Popeye sign, cramping pain and satisfaction level were found between the groups. However, tenotomy required a shorter surgical time (40.4 ± 4.0 vs. 50.4 ± 5.9 min, P < 0.001) and resulted in faster pain relief (3.1 ± 1.8 vs. 4.8 ± 1.9, P < 0.001).

Conclusion: Both tenotomy and tenodesis are effective and equal for the treatment of long head biceps lesions. plain_language_summary:
Doctors were trying to figure out the best way to fix a problem with the biceps muscle in the shoulder, which is sometimes hurt along with a torn rotator cuff (the muscles around the shoulder joint).  They looked at two different ways to fix it:  one way (tenotomy) cuts the muscle, and the other (tenodesis) moves and reattaches it.

They picked 151 people over 55 who had both problems and randomly put them into two groups.  One group got the cut method, and the other got the reattach method.  They checked how well each person did after surgery, looking at things like how long it took to recover, how much pain they had, and how strong their arm got.

After a while, they found that both ways worked about the same in the long run.  The people who had the muscle cut had less pain and a shorter surgery time.

So, the doctors learned that both ways are good, but cutting the muscle might be better because it's faster and less painful in the short term for older people with these shoulder problems.

Title: Procalcitonin guidance to reduce antibiotic treatment of lower respiratory tract infection in children and adolescents (ProPAED): a randomized controlled trial.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: focus on + secondary endpoint, make excuse for negative primary outcome
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Antibiotics are overused in children and adolescents with lower respiratory tract infection (LRTI). Serum-procalcitonin (PCT) can be used to guide treatment when bacterial infection is suspected. Its role in pediatric LRTI is unclear.

METHODS: Between 01/2009 and 02/2010 we randomized previously healthy patients 1 month to 18 years old presenting with LRTI to the emergency departments of two pediatric hospitals in Switzerland to receive antibiotics either according to a PCT guidance algorithm established for adult LRTI or standard care clinical guidelines. In intention-to-treat analyses, antibiotic prescribing rate, duration of antibiotic treatment, and number of days with impairment of daily activities within 14 days of randomization were compared between the two groups.

RESULTS: In total 337 children, mean age 3.8 years (range 0.1-18), were included. Antibiotic prescribing rates were not significantly different in PCT guided patients compared to controls (OR 1.26; 95% CI 0.81, 1.95). Mean duration of antibiotic exposure was reduced from 6.3 to 4.5 days under PCT guidance (-1.8 days; 95% CI -3.1, -0.5; P = 0.039) for all LRTI and from 9.1 to 5.7 days for pneumonia (-3.4 days 95% CI -4.9, -1.7; P<0.001). There was no apparent difference in impairment of daily activities between PCT guided and control patients.

CONCLUSION: PCT guidance reduced antibiotic exposure by reducing the duration of antibiotic treatment, while not affecting the antibiotic prescribing rate. The latter may be explained by the low baseline prescribing rate in Switzerland for pediatric LRTI and the choice of an inappropriately low PCT cut-off level for this population.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine doctors are giving kids medicine (antibiotics) for a lung infection (LRTI).  Sometimes, they give it even when it's not needed.  This study looked at whether a special test called procalcitonin (PCT) could help doctors decide when to give antibiotics.

The researchers gave some kids antibiotics based on the PCT test, and other kids got antibiotics based on the usual doctor's guidelines.  They tracked how often antibiotics were given, how long they were given, and how much the kids' daily lives were affected.

The results showed that using the PCT test helped reduce how long kids had to take antibiotics, especially for pneumonia.  But the test didn't change how often antibiotics were given in the first place.  This might be because doctors in Switzerland already weren't giving antibiotics very often for this type of infection, and the PCT test's rules were not quite right for kids.  The kids' daily lives weren't affected much either way.

plain_language_summary:
Imagine you have a sore throat or a cough (that's a lower respiratory tract infection, or LRTI).  Doctors sometimes give antibiotics to treat infections caused by bacteria.  But sometimes, they're given even when the infection is caused by a virus, which antibiotics don't help.

This study looked at whether a special test called serum-procalcitonin (PCT) could help doctors decide if a child needed antibiotics for an LRTI.  They gave some kids antibiotics based on the PCT test results, and other kids got antibiotics based on the usual doctor's guidelines.

The study found that the PCT test didn't help doctors give antibiotics more wisely.  The kids who got antibiotics based on the PCT test still got antibiotics about as often as the kids who got them based on the usual guidelines.  However, the kids who got antibiotics based on the PCT test got antibiotics for a shorter time (about 2 days less).  The study didn't look at whether the PCT test was safer.

Title: Randomized trial of hemiarthroplasty versus internal fixation for femoral neck fractures: no differences at 6 years.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Other
abstract:
Background: Hemiarthroplasty has been shown superior to internal fixation for displaced femoral neck fractures (FNF) in the first 2 years. However, there are unanswered questions about the performance of hemiarthroplasty over the longer term compared with internal fixation.

Methods: A total of 222 consecutive patients older than 60 years, including those cognitively impaired, with FNF were randomized to either internal fixation with two parallel screws or bipolar hemiarthroplasty. At a minimum follow-up of 4.9 years (mean, 5.9 years; range, 4.9-7.2 years), 68 of the 70 surviving patients were examined by a study nurse and study physiotherapist blinded to initial treatment. Questionnaires on hip function (Harris hip score), quality of life (Eq5D), and activity of daily living function (Barthel ADL) were administered.

Results: The mean survival of the groups was similar with 66.4% (73 of 110) of the patients undergoing hemiarthroplasty and 70.5% (79 of 112) of the patients undergoing internal fixation having died since surgery (p = 0.51). Only 12 of 31 living patients in the internal fixation group had retained their native hips at a mean of 6 years. Between 2 and 6 years, there were two new major reoperations (both in the internal fixation group, for avascular necrosis and deep wound infection). The mean Harris hip score was 66 (SD 19) and 67 (SD 20) in the internal fixation and hemiarthroplasty groups, respectively (p = 0.96). The mean Eq5D index was 0.50 (SD 0.40) in the internal fixation group and 0.34 (SD 0.36) in the hemiarthroplasty group (p = 0.10). Function in terms of ADLs was comparable between the groups; of the patients in the internal fixation group, 42% reported good function on the Barthel ADL index, and the corresponding number in the hemiarthroplasty group was 51% (p = 0.44).

Conclusions: The treatment with hemiarthroplasty was not more effective than internal fixation in patients with displaced femoral neck fractures.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine you have a broken hip.  Doctors have two ways to fix it:  a hemiarthroplasty (replacing part of the hip joint) or internal fixation (using screws to hold the broken bones together).

Early on, replacing part of the hip seemed better.  But, doctors weren't sure if it was still the best choice after a few years.

So, they did a big study.  They followed 222 people who had broken hips and were over 60.  They randomly put half of them in each group (hemiarthroplasty or internal fixation).  They checked on them for a long time, at least 4.9 years, on average 5.9 years.

They looked at how well each person's hip worked, how their quality of life was, and how well they could do everyday things.  They also looked at how many people died after the surgery.

The results?  There wasn't much difference between the two ways of fixing the hip.  Both groups had similar death rates, similar hip function scores, similar quality of life scores, and similar ability to do daily tasks.  In fact, the people who got the internal fixation had a lot more problems later on.

So, the study showed that replacing part of the hip wasn't better than using screws to hold the bones together in the long run.

Title: Randomized trial of hemiarthroplasty versus internal fixation for femoral neck fractures: no differences at 6 years.

abstract:
Background: Hemiarthroplasty has been shown superior to internal fixation for displaced femoral neck fractures (FNF) in the first 2 years. However, there are unanswered questions about the performance of hemiarthroplasty over the longer term compared with internal fixation.

Questions/purposes: We sought to compare hemiarthroplasty with internal fixation in terms of (1) outcomes scores for pain, hip function, and quality of life at a minimum of 5 years after surgery in a randomized trial. A secondary purpose was to compare (2) patient survival and (3) frequency of reoperation in the two groups.

Methods: A total of 222 consecutive patients older than 60 years, including those cognitively impaired, with FNF were randomized to either internal fixation with two parallel screws or bipolar hemiarthroplasty. Questionnaires on hip function (Harris hip score), quality of life (Eq5D), and activity of daily living function (Barthel ADL) were administered. The Barthel ADL index score was split into good function (score 95 or 100) and reduced function (score below 95).

Results: The mean survival of the groups was similar with 66.4% (73 of 110) of the patients undergoing hemiarthroplasty and 70.5% (79 of 112) of the patients undergoing internal fixation having died since surgery (p = 0.51). Only 12 of 31 living patients in the internal fixation group had retained their native hips at a mean of 6 years. Function in terms of ADLs was comparable between the groups; of the patients in the internal fixation group, 42% reported good function on the Barthel ADL index, and the corresponding number in the hemiarthroplasty group was 51% (p = 0.44).

Conclusions: Hemiarthroplasty has predictable and good long-term results after FNF and is the treatment of choice compared with internal fixation. Further studies will evaluate if total hip arthroplasty has advantages over hemiarthroplasty in patients with fracture with long life expectancy.

So, they did a big study.  They followed a bunch of people who were over 60 and had broken hips, randomly putting them into either the screw-fix or the replacement-part fix group.  They checked on them for at least 5 years, asking about their pain, how well their hip worked, and how their overall health was.

The results?  After a long time, both ways of fixing the hip worked pretty much the same.  There wasn't a big difference in how much pain people had, how well their hips worked, or how long they lived.  However, the hip replacement group had slightly better quality of life scores and a slightly higher percentage of people with good daily function.  Importantly, the screw-fix group had more problems with the hip needing to be fixed again.

So, the study suggests that replacing part of the hip is probably a good choice for a long-term fix for broken hips in older people.  But, they might need to look at a different way to fix the hip if people are expected to live a very long

Title: The effect of infrapatellar fat pad excision on complications after minimally invasive TKA: a randomized controlled trial.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim benefit based on (+) secondary endpoint
abstract:
Background: The infrapatellar fat pad is one of the structures that obscures exposure in minimally invasive total knee arthroplasty (MIS TKA). Most MIS TKA surgeons (and many surgeons who use other approaches as well) excise the fat pad for better exposure of the knee. There is still controversy about the result of fat pad excision on patella baja, pain, and function.

Questions/purposes: In the setting of a randomized controlled trial, we sought to determine whether infrapatellar fat pad excision during MIS TKA causes (1) patellar tendon shortening (as measured by patella baja); (2) increased anterior knee pain; (3) decreases in the Knee Society Score or functional subscore; or (4) more patella-related complications.

Methods: We randomized 90 patients undergoing MIS TKA at one institution into two groups. In one group, 45 patients underwent MIS TKA with complete infrapatellar fat pad excision and in the other group, 45 patients received MIS TKA without infrapatellar fat pad excision. The patella was selectively resurfaced in these patients; there was no difference between the groups in terms of the percentage of patients whose patellae were resurfaced. We measured patellar tendon shortening, knee flexion, anterior knee pain, Knee Society Score (KSS), functional subscore, and patellar complications at preoperative and postoperative periods of 6 weeks, 3 months, 6 months, and 1 year; complete follow-up data were available on 86% of patients (77 of 90) who were enrolled.

Results: At the final follow-up, no significant differences were observed in patellar tendon shortening, KSS, functional subscore, or knee flexion in either group. Patients with their infrapatellar fat pad excised experienced more anterior knee pain (8.3% versus 0%; p = 0.03; 95% confidence interval, -0.007 to 0.174) at the end of the study. No patellar complications were found in either group.

Conclusions: The treatment of infrapatellar fat pad excision was not more effective than non-excision in patients undergoing MIS TKA in terms of patellar tendon shortening, KSS, functional subscore, or knee flexion. However, it was associated with increased anterior knee pain.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine you're fixing a broken toy.  Sometimes, there's extra stuff in the way that makes it hard to see what you need to fix.  Doctors sometimes do the same thing with knees.  

This study looked at a special way to fix knees (MIS TKA).  Sometimes, doctors remove a bit of extra fat (the infrapatellar fat pad) to get a better view.  The study wanted to see if removing this fat pad made a difference in how well the knee worked and if it caused more pain.

They split a group of people getting this knee surgery into two groups. One group had the fat pad removed, and the other group didn't.  They watched both groups for a whole year to see how their knees healed.

The results showed that removing the fat pad didn't make the knee work better or heal faster.  However, people who had the fat pad removed reported more knee pain in the front of their knee.  So, while removing the fat pad didn't hurt the knee in other ways, it did seem to cause more pain.

plain_language_summary:
Imagine your knee is like a puzzle, and doctors need a good view to put the pieces back together during a knee replacement surgery.  Sometimes, a piece of fat in the knee, called the infrapatellar fat pad, blocks the view.

Some doctors remove this fat pad to get a better look.  This study looked at whether removing this fat pad made a difference in how the knee healed.

They divided a group of people getting knee replacements into two groups. One group had the fat pad removed, and the other group didn't.  They checked on everyone for a whole year after surgery, looking at things like how much the knee hurt, how well it worked, and if there were any problems with the kneecap.

The results showed that removing the fat pad caused a small but noticeable increase in knee pain in some people.  There was no difference in how well the knee worked or in other problems.

So, the doctors learned that while removing the fat pad might help them see better during surgery, it might also cause more knee pain in some people.  They should only remove it if they really need a better view.

Title: Intraarticular analgesia versus epidural plus femoral nerve block after TKA: a randomized, double-blind trial.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim equivalence/non-inferiority versus control for a (-) endpoint
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Pain management after TKA remains challenging and the efficacy of continuously infused intraarticular anesthetics remains a controversial topic.

METHODS: Ninety-four patients undergoing unilateral TKA were prospectively randomized to receive a spinal-epidural analgesic infusion plus a single-injection FNB or a spinal anesthetic plus a continuous postoperative intraarticular infusion of 0.2% ropivacaine. All patients were blinded to their treatment with placebo saline catheters. Blinded coinvestigators collected data concerning side effect profiles (nausea, hypotension), analgesic efficacy (VAS pain scores, narcotic usage), and functional recovery (timed up and go test, quadriceps strength, WOMAC scores, Knee Society scores, early postoperative ambulatory ability, in-hospital falls). All complications and adverse events were recorded.

RESULTS: The frequency of nausea and hypertension was not different between the study groups. During the first 12 and 24 postoperative hours, the mean maximum VAS pain scores were higher in the ropivacaine group than in the epidural group (first 12 hours: 3.93 versus 1.14, respectively, p < 0.0001; 12-24 hours: 3.52 versus 1.93, respectively, p = 0.008). After 24 hours, pain scores were similar between groups. Narcotic consumption was significantly higher in the ropivacaine group on the day of surgery, but overall in-hospital narcotic usage was similar between groups. There were no clinically important differences in functional recovery between groups at any time point. No infections occurred in either group, and the frequency of complications was not different between groups.CONCLUSIONS: The treatment with a continuous intraarticular infusion of ropivacaine was not more effective than epidural analgesia plus single-injection FNB in patients undergoing TKA.
plain_language_summary:
Doctors were trying to figure out the best way to help people who have knee replacement surgery (TKA) feel less pain.  They did a test with two groups of people.

One group got medicine in their spine and around their back to numb the area.  The other group got medicine directly into their knee joint that kept flowing in.

They watched how much pain each group felt, how much medicine they needed, and how well they could move.

The results showed that the medicine in the knee didn't really work better than the medicine in the spine and back for pain relief, especially in the first few days after surgery.  Both groups needed similar amounts of pain medicine overall, and they recovered about the same.  There were no big differences in side effects either. So, the medicine in the knee wasn't a better way to manage pain after knee replacement surgery.

Title: Intraarticular analgesia versus epidural plus femoral nerve block after TKA: a randomized, double-blind trial.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim equivalence/non-inferiority versus control for a (-) endpoint
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Pain management after TKA remains challenging and the efficacy of continuously infused intraarticular anesthetics remains a controversial topic.QUESTIONS/PURPOSES: We compared the side effect profile, analgesic efficacy, and functional recovery between patients receiving a continuous intraarticular infusion of ropivacaine and patients receiving an epidural plus femoral nerve block (FNB) after TKA.

METHODS: Ninety-four patients undergoing unilateral TKA were prospectively randomized to receive a spinal-epidural analgesic infusion plus a single-injection FNB or a spinal anesthetic plus a continuous postoperative intraarticular infusion of 0.2% ropivacaine. All patients were blinded to their treatment with placebo saline catheters. Blinded coinvestigators collected data concerning side effect profiles (nausea, hypotension), analgesic efficacy (VAS pain scores, narcotic usage), and functional recovery (timed up and go test, quadriceps strength, WOMAC scores, Knee Society scores, early postoperative ambulatory ability, in-hospital falls). All complications and adverse events were recorded.

RESULTS: The frequency of nausea and hypertension was not different between the study groups. During the first 12 and 24 postoperative hours, the mean maximum VAS pain scores were higher in the ropivacaine group than in the epidural group (first 12 hours: 3.93 versus 1.14, respectively, pВ <В 0.0001; 12-24 hours: 3.52 versus 1.93, respectively, pВ =В 0.008). After 24 hours, pain scores were similar between groups. Narcotic consumption was significantly higher in the ropivacaine group on the day of surgery, but overall in-hospital narcotic usage was similar between groups. There were no clinically important differences in functional recovery between groups at any time point, but patients in the epidural group were more likely to have knee buckling (32.7% versus 6.7%, pВ =В 0.002) and delayed ambulation (16.3% versus 0.0%, pВ =В 0.006) than patients in the ropivacaine group, though not in-hospital falls. No infections occurred in either group, and the frequency of complications was not different between groups.CONCLUSIONS: A continuous intraarticular infusion of ropivacaine can be recommended as a safe, effective alternative to epidural analgesia plus single-injection FNB after TKA. Improved analgesic efficacy in the group that received epidural analgesia plus single-injection FNB must be weighed against the disadvantage of a higher likelihood of knee buckling and delayed ambulation with that treatment approach.LEVEL OF EVIDENCE: Level I, therapeutic study. See Instructions for Authors for a complete description of levels of evidence.

Title: Gap balancing sacrifices joint-line maintenance to improve gap symmetry: a randomized controlled trial comparing gap balancing and measured resection.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim benefit based on (+) secondary endpoint
abstract: A total knee arthroplasty can be completed using two techniques; measured resection or gap balancing. A prospective blinded randomized controlled trial was completed with 103 patients randomized to measured resection (n = 52) or gap balancing (n = 51). Primary outcome measure was femoral component rotation. Secondary outcome measures were joint-line change, gap symmetry and function and quality-of-life outcomes. Gap balancing resulted in a significantly raised joint-line compared to measured resection. Gap symmetry was significantly better using gap balancing. Functional outcomes and quality-of-life were not significantly different at 24 months. Using computer navigation, gap balancing significantly raises the joint-line in order to improve gap symmetry. This does not result in a clinical difference in function or quality of life at 24 months.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine you have a broken toy, and you have two ways to fix it:  one way is like carefully measuring how much to fix it, and the other way is like making sure the pieces fit together well.

Doctors did a test with 103 people who had knee problems.  They randomly chose some people to get one fix (measured resection) and others to get the other fix (gap balancing).

They checked how well the knee worked after 2 years.

The "gap balancing" fix made the knee joint line higher, and the pieces fit together better.  But, it didn't make the people feel any better or have any more usable knees after 2 years.

Using a special tool (like a computer), the "gap balancing" fix made the knee joint line higher to make it fit better.  But, it didn't make a real difference in how well the people could use their knees or how happy they were with their knees after 2 years.

plain_language_summary:
Imagine you have a broken toy, and you need to fix it.  Doctors have two ways to fix a broken knee joint:  one way is like measuring and cutting the pieces exactly right (measured resection), and the other way is like adjusting the space between the pieces (gap balancing).

Researchers did a test with 103 people who had broken knee joints.  They randomly put half of them in each group (measured resection or gap balancing).  They checked how well the knee worked after 2 years.

The gap balancing method made the joint space a little too big, compared to the measured resection method.  But, after two years, both groups were about the same in how well their knees worked and how happy they were with their knees.  So, the gap balancing method wasn't better than the measured resection method.

Title: A randomized controlled trial of storytelling as a communication tool.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim benefit based on (+) secondary endpoint
abstract:
INTRODUCTION: Stories may be an effective tool to communicate with patients because of their ability to engage the reader. Our objective was to evaluate the effectiveness of story booklets compared to standard information sheets for parents of children attending the emergency department (ED) with a child with croup.

METHODS: Parents were randomized to receive story booklets (n=208) or standard information sheets (n=205) during their ED visit. The primary outcome was change in anxiety between triage to ED discharge as measured by the State-Trait Anxiety Inventory. Follow-up telephone interviews were conducted at 1 and 3 days after discharge, then every other day until 9 days (or until resolution of symptoms), and at 1 year. Secondary outcomes included expected future anxiety, event impact, parental knowledge, satisfaction, decision regret, healthcare utilization, and time to symptom resolution.

RESULTS: There was no significant difference in the primary outcome of change in parental anxiety between recruitment and ED discharge (change of 5 points for the story group vs. 6 points for the comparison group, p=0.78). The story group showed significantly greater decision regret regarding their decision to go to the ED (p<0.001): 6.7% of the story group vs. 1.5% of the comparison group strongly disagreed with the statement "I would go for the same choice if I had to do it over again." No other outcomes were different between study groups.CONCLUSIONS: The treatment with story booklets was not more effective than standard information sheets in reducing parental anxiety in parents of children with croup. 

TRIAL REGISTRATION: Current Controlled Trials, ISRCTN39642997 (http://www.controlled-trials.com/ISRCTN39642997).
plain_language_summary:
Imagine you're sick and need to go to the hospital.  Doctors give parents information about what's happening.  This study looked at two ways to give that information:  one was a regular sheet of information, and the other was a booklet with stories.

They gave one group of parents the story booklet and another group the regular information sheet.  They asked the parents how worried they were before and after they left the hospital.  They also asked them questions about how they felt about their decision to go to the hospital.

The study found that the story booklets didn't make parents any less worried than the regular information sheets.  However, the parents who got the story booklets were more likely to say they regretted going to the hospital.  So, while the stories didn't help with worry, they might have made some parents feel worse about their decision.
