abstract:
Background: Opioid analgesics have been a standard modality for postoperative pain management after total knee arthroplasty (TKA) but are also associated with increased risk of nausea, pruritus, vomiting, respiratory depression, prolonged ileus, and cognitive dysfunction. There is still a need for a method of anesthesia that can deliver effective long-term postoperative pain relief without incurring the high cost and health burden of opioids and nerve blocks.

Questions/purposes: (1) Is liposomal bupivacaine-based periarticular injection (PAI) more effective than morphine-based spinal anesthesia or ropivacaine-based PAI in controlling postoperative pain after TKA? (2) Do patients treated with liposomal bupivacaine-based PAI experience fewer opioid-related adverse events compared with patients treated with morphine-based spinal anesthesia or ropivacaine-based PAI in controlling postoperative pain after TKA?

Methods: This multicenter, blind trial randomized 119 patients undergoing TKA with spinal anesthesia to receive spinal anesthesia plus periarticular injection with liposomal bupivacaine (40 patients), spinal anesthesia with bupivacaine plus intrathecal morphine (41 patients) but no liposomal bupivacaine injection, or spinal anesthesia with bupivacaine (38 patients) and no liposomal bupivacaine injection. The two groups that did not receive periarticular liposomal bupivacaine did receive periarticular injection with ropivacaine, and all three groups had ketorolac (30 mg) plus epinephrine (1:1000) in the periarticular injections. Patients in all three groups received identical perioperative multimodal analgesic and antiemetic drugs. All patients were analyzed in the group to which they were randomized and no patients were lost to followup. The primary study endpoints were visual analog score (VAS) for pain and narcotic use during postoperative day 1. Secondary endpoints included side effects associated with narcotic administration during the hospital stay.

Results: Mean VAS pain in the liposomal bupivacaine PAI group was lower than that for the ropivacaine PAI group at 6 hours (1.8 ± 2.1 versus 3.3 ± 2.3, p = 0.005, mean difference: 1.5, 95% confidence interval [CI], 0.5-2.5) and 12 hours (1.5 ± 2.0 versus 3.3 ± 2.4, p < 0.001, mean difference: 1.8, 95% CI, 0.8-2.8) after surgery. The morphine spinal group had lower pain compared with the liposomal bupivacaine PAI group at 6 hours (0.9 ± 1.8 versus 1.8 ± 2.1, p = 0.035, mean difference: 1.0, 95% CI, 0.1-1.8), but there was no difference at 12 hours (0.8 ± 1.5 versus 1.5 ± 2.0, p = 0.086, mean difference: 0.7, 95% CI, -0.1 to 1.5). The magnitude of the differences at 6 and 12 hours are near the lower end of minimal clinically important differences reported in the literature, and thus the improvement shown in this study may only represent a small clinical improvement. Both the liposomal bupivacaine group (13% [five of 40]) and the ropivacaine group (5% [two of 38]) had fewer incidents of itching (pruritus) than the spinal morphine group (38% [15 of 41]) (p = 0.001).

Conclusions: This prospective multicenter three-arm blind randomized controlled trial showed potentially improved pain control at 6 and 12 hours in the liposomal bupivacaine and intrathecal morphine groups compared with the ropivacaine group at the cost of much higher incidences of pruritus (itching) in the intrathecal morphine group. Based on these results, we prefer the use of PAI with liposomal bupivacaine as an alternative to spinal anesthesia with intrathecal morphine as a result of similar postoperative pain control and the potential for reducing adverse events.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine you're getting a new knee.  Doctors use medicine to help with the pain after surgery.  Some medicines, like opioids, work well but can cause side effects like nausea, itching, and trouble breathing.  Other medicines, like nerve blocks, can also help, but they might not work as well.

This study looked at three different ways to control pain after knee surgery.  One used a special type of medicine called liposomal bupivacaine, another used a medicine called morphine, and the third used a different type of medicine called ropivacaine.

The study tested how well each medicine worked at controlling pain for the first two days after surgery.  They also looked at how many side effects each medicine caused.

The results showed that the liposomal bupivacaine worked almost as well as the morphine, but caused fewer side effects like itching.  The ropivacaine worked less well than the other two.

So, the study suggests that liposomal bupivacaine might be a good alternative to morphine for controlling pain after knee surgery, because it works pretty well and has fewer side effects.

Title: Irradiated Hamstring Tendon Allograft Versus Autograft for Anatomic Double-Bundle Anterior Cruciate Ligament Reconstruction: Midterm Clinical Outcomes.
Journal: The American Journal of Sports Medicine
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Most studies on grafts for anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) reconstruction (ACLR) have been of autografts or nonirradiated allografts with a single-bundle (SB) technique. Outcome reports evaluating anatomic double-bundle (DB) ACLR with a hamstring tendon autograft versus irradiated allograft are rare.

PURPOSE: To compare the clinical outcomes of arthroscopic anatomic DB ACLR with a hamstring tendon autograft versus irradiated allograft.

STUDY DESIGN: Randomized controlled trial; Level of evidence, 2.

METHODS: Between 2008 and 2009, a total of 107 patients undergoing arthroscopic DB ACLR were prospectively randomized consecutively into 1 of 2 groups (autograft [Auto] group and irradiated allograft [Ir-Allo] group). All the surgical procedures were performed by the same senior surgeon using the DB reconstruction technique. All irradiated hamstring tendon allografts were sterilized with 2.5 Mrad of irradiation before distribution and were obtained from a single certified tissue bank. Graft fixation on the femoral side was by an Endobutton, and on the tibial side by a bioabsorbable interference screw augmented with a staple. The same rehabilitation protocol was applied to all patients. Before surgery and at a mean of 6.9 years of follow-up, patients were evaluated by the same observer according to objective and subjective clinical evaluations including detailed history, physical examination, radiography, functional knee ligament testing, KT-2000 arthrometer testing, Harner vertical jump and Daniel 1-legged hop tests, Lysholm score, Tegner score, International Knee Documentation Committee (IKDC) standard evaluation form, and Cincinnati knee score.

RESULTS: A total of 83 patients (Auto: n = 40 [mean age, 29.2 ± 6.9 years]; Ir-Allo: n = 43 [mean age, 28.6 ± 7.2 years]) fulfilled follow-up and clinical evaluations. No significant differences were found between the 2 groups according to the overall IKDC functional and subjective evaluations as well as testing of activity levels. Significant between-group differences were found when comparing the results at final follow-up according to the Lachman test, anterior drawer test, pivot-shift test, and KT-2000 arthrometer measurements (P < .001). The rate of laxity (side-to-side difference >5 mm) with an irradiated allograft (30.2%) was higher than that with an autograft (7.5%) (P < .001). The failure rate in the Ir-Allo group (30.2%) was higher than that in the Auto group (7.5%) (P < .001). There were 10.0% (4/40) of patients in the Auto group and 32.6% (19/43) of patients in the Ir-Allo group who had arthritic progression (P < .05).

CONCLUSION: The treatment with a hamstring tendon autograft was not more effective than an irradiated allograft in patients undergoing DB ACLR.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your knee is like a hinge on a door.  Sometimes, that hinge gets damaged, and doctors need to fix it.  One way to fix it is by using a new hinge part (a graft).  This study looked at two different ways to make this new hinge: using a part from your own body (autograft) or using a part from someone else (allograft), that's been treated to be safe.

The study wanted to see which way worked better for a special kind of knee repair (double-bundle ACLR).  They took a bunch of people who needed this repair and randomly put them into two groups: one got the autograft, and the other got the treated allograft.  They all got the same surgery and the same care after.

After a long time (almost 7 years), they checked how well the knees were doing.  They looked at things like how much the knee moved, how much pain the people felt, and how well they could use their knee.

The results showed that the autograft (using a part from your own body) was better.  The knees that got the treated allograft were more likely to have problems, like being loose or getting arthritis later on.  So, using a part from your own body was better for fixing the knee in this study.

Title: Irradiated Hamstring Tendon Allograft Versus Autograft for Anatomic Double-Bundle Anterior Cruciate Ligament Reconstruction: Midterm Clinical Outcomes.
Journal: The American Journal of Sports Medicine

BACKGROUND: Most studies on grafts for anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) reconstruction (ACLR) have been of autografts or nonirradiated allografts with a single-bundle (SB) technique. PURPOSE: To compare the clinical outcomes of arthroscopic anatomic DB ACLR with a hamstring tendon autograft versus irradiated allograft.

STUDY DESIGN: Randomized controlled trial; Level of evidence, 2.

METHODS: Between 2008 and 2009, a total of 107 patients undergoing arthroscopic DB ACLR were prospectively randomized consecutively into 1 of 2 groups (autograft [Auto] group and irradiated allograft [Ir-Allo] group). Graft fixation on the femoral side was by an Endobutton, and on the tibial side by a bioabsorbable interference screw augmented with a staple. Before surgery and at a mean of 6.9 years of follow-up, patients were evaluated by the same observer according to objective and subjective clinical evaluations including detailed history, physical examination, radiography, functional knee ligament testing, KT-2000 arthrometer testing, Harner vertical jump and Daniel 1-legged hop tests, Lysholm score, Tegner score, International Knee Documentation Committee (IKDC) standard evaluation form, and Cincinnati knee score.

RESULTS: A total of 83 patients (Auto: n = 40 [mean age, 29.2 В± 6.9 years]; Ir-Allo: n = 43 [mean age, 28.6 В± 7.2 years]) fulfilled follow-up and clinical evaluations. No significant differences were found between the 2 groups according to the overall IKDC functional and subjective evaluations as well as testing of activity levels. Significant between-group differences were found when comparing the results at final follow-up according to the Lachman test, anterior drawer test, pivot-shift test, and KT-2000 arthrometer measurements (P < .001). Most importantly, 87.5% of patients in the Auto group and 34.9% in the Ir-Allo group had a side-to-side difference <3 mm. The rate of laxity (side-to-side difference >5 mm) with an irradiated allograft (30.2%) was higher than that with an autograft (7.5%) (P < .001). The failure rate in the Ir-Allo group (30.2%) was higher than that in the Auto group (7.5%) (P < .001). Anterior and rotational stability decreased significantly in the Ir-Allo group; patients in the Ir-Allo group also had a shorter operation time. plain_language_summary:
Imagine your knee is like a hinge on a door.  Sometimes, that hinge gets damaged, and doctors need to fix it.  One way to fix it is by using a new hinge (a graft).  This study looked at two different ways to make this new hinge: using a piece of your own knee (autograft) or using a piece of someone else's knee (allograft) that's been treated with special radiation.

The study tested which way worked better for a specific type of knee repair (double-bundle ACLR).  They followed a group of people who had this surgery using either their own tissue or the treated tissue from someone else.  They checked how well their knees worked over a long time (6.9 years).

The results showed that using your own tissue (autograft) was much better.  People who got the autograft had less problems with their knee being loose or unstable, and they had a much lower chance of needing another surgery.  The treated tissue (allograft) had more problems with knee instability and a higher chance of needing another surgery.  The study also found that the autograft group had less knee arthritis.

Basically, the study said that using your own tissue is a much better way to fix this type of knee injury.

Title: No Effects of Early Viscosupplementation After Arthroscopic Partial Meniscectomy: A Randomized Controlled Trial.
Journal: The American Journal of Sports Medicine
abstract:
BACKGROUND: The management of the postoperative period after knee arthroscopic surgery may be challenging because surgical trauma deeply alters the joint microenvironment, causing the release of several catabolic molecules and proinflammatory factors that might slow down functional recovery. The possibility of using hyaluronic acid (HA) to promote postoperative pain relief and expedite functional improvement seems attractive, considering its biological properties.

PURPOSE: The aim of the present double-blind randomized controlled trial was to evaluate the effects, in terms of pain control and functional recovery, provided by a single HA injection performed at the end of arthroscopic meniscectomy.

STUDY DESIGN: Randomized controlled trial; Level of evidence, 1.

METHODS: A total of 90 patients, 18 to 55 years old, were included according to the following criteria: (1) chronic, symptomatic meniscal tears requiring partial resection; (2) a healthy contralateral knee; (3) no previous surgery on the index knee; and (4) no other concurrent articular lesions requiring surgical treatment (eg, cartilage or ligament injuries). Patients were randomized into 2 treatment groups: one underwent meniscectomy alone, whereas the other also received an injection of 3 mL HA at the end of the procedure. All patients were evaluated at baseline and then at 15, 30, 60, and 180 days using the following tools: International Knee Documentation Committee (IKDC) subjective, Knee injury and Osteoarthritis Outcome Score (KOOS), visual analog scale (VAS) for pain, VAS for general health status, and Tegner scores. The transpatellar circumference and active and passive ranges of motion were also recorded during the follow-up evaluations.

RESULTS: No major adverse events were reported using HA postoperatively. A statistically significant increase in all the clinical scores was reported in both treatment groups, but no significant intergroup difference was documented at any follow-up evaluation. No difference was observed also in the objective measurements. The mean time to return to full sports activity was not different between groups, and a comparable satisfaction rate was recorded in both treatment groups.

CONCLUSION: Early postoperative viscosupplementation did not provide significant clinical benefits after arthroscopic meniscectomy. Despite the lack of major adverse events, the administration of a single HA injection at the end of the surgical procedure is not a successful strategy to provide either faster functional recovery or symptomatic improvement after meniscectomy.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your knee is like a playground.  Sometimes, parts of the playground (like the cartilage or the meniscus) get hurt.  Doctors sometimes fix these parts with surgery, like fixing a broken swing set.

This study looked at a special medicine, hyaluronic acid (HA), to see if it could help knees heal faster after a surgery called arthroscopic meniscectomy (fixing a torn part of the knee).

They took 90 kids with knee problems and divided them into two groups. One group got the surgery and then a shot of HA. The other group just got the surgery.  They checked how the knees felt and worked over time (15 days, 30 days, 60 days, and 6 months).

The study found that the HA shot didn't really help the knees heal any faster or feel any better.  Both groups got better, but there wasn't a big difference between them.  The kids who got the HA shot didn't have any serious problems from it either.

So, the researchers learned that giving HA after this type of knee surgery isn't a good way to speed up healing or make the pain go away.

Title: No Effects of Early Viscosupplementation After Arthroscopic Partial Meniscectomy: A Randomized Controlled Trial.
Journal: The American Journal of Sports Medicine
abstract:
BACKGROUND: The management of the postoperative period after knee arthroscopic surgery may be challenging because surgical trauma deeply alters the joint microenvironment, causing the release of several catabolic molecules and proinflammatory factors that might slow down functional recovery.

PURPOSE: The aim of the present double-blind randomized controlled trial was to evaluate the effects, in terms of pain control and functional recovery, provided by a single HA injection performed at the end of arthroscopic meniscectomy.

STUDY DESIGN: Randomized controlled trial; Level of evidence, 1.

METHODS: A total of 90 patients, 18 to 55 years old, were included according to the following criteria: (1) chronic, symptomatic meniscal tears requiring partial resection; (2) a healthy contralateral knee; (3) no previous surgery on the index knee; and (4) no other concurrent articular lesions requiring surgical treatment (eg, cartilage or ligament injuries). Patients were randomized into 2 treatment groups: one underwent meniscectomy alone, whereas the other also received an injection of 3 mL HA at the end of the procedure. All patients were evaluated at baseline and then at 15, 30, 60, and 180 days using the following tools: International Knee Documentation Committee (IKDC) subjective, Knee injury and Osteoarthritis Outcome Score (KOOS), visual analog scale (VAS) for pain, VAS for general health status, and Tegner scores. The transpatellar circumference and active and passive ranges of motion were also recorded during the follow-up evaluations.

RESULTS: No major adverse events were reported using HA postoperatively. A statistically significant increase in all the clinical scores was reported in both treatment groups, but no significant intergroup difference was documented at any follow-up evaluation. No difference was observed also in the objective measurements. The mean time to return to full sports activity was not different between groups, and a comparable satisfaction rate was recorded in both treatment groups.

CONCLUSION: The treatment with HA was not more effective than meniscectomy alone in patients with postoperative recovery after arthroscopic meniscectomy.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your knee is like a playground.  Sometimes, parts of the playground (your knee) get hurt, and need a little fix-up.  Doctors sometimes do a surgery called arthroscopic meniscectomy to fix a torn part of the knee.

This study looked at whether a special injection (of a substance called HA) after this surgery helped people recover faster and feel better.

They took 90 people who needed this surgery and split them into two groups.  One group got the surgery and the injection, and the other group just got the surgery.

They checked how the people felt and moved their knees at different times after the surgery (15 days, 30 days, 60 days, and 6 months).  They used special tests to measure pain, how well the knee worked, and how satisfied the people were.

The results showed that the injection didn't make a big difference.  Both groups got better, but there wasn't a clear winner between the injection and the surgery alone.  Both groups recovered about the same.

Title: Navigation is Equal to Estimation by Eye and Palpation in Preventing Psoas Impingement in THA.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim benefit based on (+) subgroup analysis
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Iliopsoas tendon impingement is a potential cause of persistent groin pain and reduced functional outcome after total hip arthroplasty (THA). Estimation by eye and palpation is the standard procedure to intraoperatively assess the distance of the cup to the anterior rim. Novel technologies such as imageless navigation enable intraoperative measurements of the cup in relation to the psoas tendon and bony landmarks.QUESTIONS/PURPOSES: We aimed to determine whether psoas impingement can be reduced using imageless navigation compared with the standard technique.

METHODS: This study is a reanalysis of data from a randomized controlled trial evaluating navigation for THA; 135 patients were randomized for surgery with or without the use of imageless navigation. The risk for anterior protrusion of the cup above the acetabular rim and potential psoas impingement, defined as an overhang of the cup above the anterior acetabular rim as measured on postoperative CT, was either controlled with the help of navigation or standard control by eye and palpation intraoperatively. Postoperatively, the anterior protrusion of the cup above the acetabular rim was measured on three-dimensional (3-D) CT by a blinded, external institute. The primary outcome was the distance of the cup below the anterosuperior and anteroinferior acetabular rim. Altogether, 123 data sets were available for primary analysis and 115 were available for 1-year follow-up.

RESULTS: There was no difference, with the numbers available, between the navigated and the control groups in terms of the mean distance of the cup below the anterosuperior acetabular rim (3.9 mm; -5.3 to 12.6 mm versus 4.4 mm; -7.9 to 13.7 mm; p = 0.72) or the anteroinferior acetabular rim (4.7 mm; -6.2 to 14.8 mm versus 4.2 mm; -7.1 to 16.3 mm; p = 0.29). There was no difference, with the numbers available, in terms of the proportion of cups with anterior overhang (7%, four of 57 versus 15%, 10 of 66; p = 0.16). With the numbers available, there were no differences observed in clinical scores between groups with and without anterior cup protrusion.CONCLUSIONS: The treatment using imageless navigation was not more effective than estimation by eye and palpation in preventing potential psoas impingement in patients undergoing THA.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine you're building a house, and you need to put a special part (the "cup") in the hip.  Sometimes, this part can bump into a muscle (the "psoas tendon") and cause pain.  Doctors used to guess where to put the cup, but now some use a special tool (imageless navigation) to measure and make sure the cup doesn't hit the muscle.

This study looked at whether using this special tool made a difference in how much the cup bumped into the muscle.  They followed a bunch of people who had this surgery, some with the tool and some without.  They measured how far the cup was from the muscle after the surgery.

The results showed that the special tool didn't make a big difference.  The cup was about the same distance from the muscle in both groups of people.  So, the tool didn't seem to help prevent the cup from hitting the muscle and causing pain.

Title: Navigation is Equal to Estimation by Eye and Palpation in Preventing Psoas Impingement in THA.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim benefit based on (+) subgroup analysis
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Iliopsoas tendon impingement is one possible reason for persistent groin pain and diminished functional outcome after THA. So far, estimation by eye and palpation is the standard procedure to intraoperatively assess the distance of the cup to the anterior rim. However, novel technologies such as imageless navigation enable intraoperative measurements of the cup in relation to the psoas tendon and bony landmarks.QUESTIONS/PURPOSES: We asked whether psoas impingement (1) can be reduced using imageless navigation compared with the standard technique and (2) is associated with specific patient characteristics. Furthermore, we investigated (3) if anterior cup protrusion (overhang) is associated with lower outcome scores for pain and function.

METHODS: The current study is a reanalysis of data from a randomized controlled trial evaluating navigation for THA; 135 patients were randomized for surgery with or without the use of imageless navigation. The risk for anterior protrusion of the cup above the acetabular rim and thus potential psoas impingement, defined as an overhang of the cup above the anterior acetabular rim as measured on postoperative CT, was either controlled with the help of navigation or standard control by eye and palpation intraoperatively. Postoperatively, the anterior protrusion of the cup above the acetabular rim was measured on three-dimensional (3-D) CT by a blinded, external institute. In addition to patient-related outcome measures, the Harris hip score, Hip disability and Osteoarthritis Outcome Score, and EuroQol were obtained 1 year postoperatively. Altogether 123 data sets were available for primary analysis and 115 were available for 1-year followup.

RESULTS: There was no difference, with the numbers available, between the navigated and the control groups in terms of the mean distance of the cup below the anterosuperior acetabular rim (3.9 mm; -5.3 to 12.6 mm versus 4.4 mm; -7.9 to 13.7 mm; p = 0.72) or the anteroinferior acetabular rim (4.7 mm; -6.2 to 14.8 mm versus 4.2 mm; -7.1 to 16.3 mm; p = 0.29). There was no difference, with the numbers available, in terms of the proportion of cups with anterior overhang (7%, four of 57 versus, 15%, 10 of 66; p = 0.16), respectively. After controlling for potential confounding variables such as cup inclination, cup size, patient age, BMI, stage of arthritis, and length of skin incision, we found cup anteversion (hazard ratio [HR], 0.87; 95% CI, 0.81-0.93; p < 0.001) and female sex (HR, 3.88; 95% CI, 1.01-14.93; p = 0.049) were associated with a propensity to potential psoas impingement. With the numbers available, there were no differences observed in clinical scores between groups with and without anterior cup protrusion.CONCLUSIONS: We found no differences between imageless navigation and estimation by eye and palpation in preventing potential psoas impingement. Despite the comparable clinical outcome for patients with and without cup protrusion, the orthopaedic surgeon should be especially aware of propensity for psoas impingement in women.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine you're fixing a broken toy car.  Sometimes, when you put the parts back together, one part might bump into another and cause problems.  This study is about a similar problem in hip replacement surgery.

Doctors sometimes put a new hip socket (cup) into a person's hip.  Sometimes, this cup can bump into a muscle called the psoas tendon, causing pain.  The study looked at two ways to put the cup in: one way is just looking and feeling (like guessing where to put a piece), and the other way uses special tools to measure exactly where to put the cup.

The researchers wanted to see if the special tools made a difference in how often the cup bumped into the psoas tendon.  They also wanted to see if certain things, like a person's age, gender, or how bad their arthritis was, made a difference.

They looked at 135 people who had hip replacements.  Some got the special tools, and some didn't.  They measured how the cup fit in each person's hip after the surgery.  They also asked the people how much pain they had and how well they could use their hip.

The results showed that the special tools didn't make a big difference in how often the cup bumped into the psoas tendon.  But, the study did find that women were more likely to have the cup bump into the psoas tendon.

Title: What Is the Best Treatment before Bariatric Surgery? Exercise, Exercise and Group Therapy, or Conventional Waiting: a Randomized Controlled Trial.
abstract:
Objective: This trial's objective was to investigate the effect of an exercise program with and without cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT), compared by a control group, on weight, functional capacity, and cardiometabolic profile of morbidly obese individuals while waiting for bariatric surgery.

Materials and methods: This randomized controlled trial investigated the effect of a 4-month low-intensity exercise program (two weekly sessions of 25 min each) on 66 morbidly obese individuals awaiting bariatric surgery. Participants were randomly divided into three groups: EXER, exercise program; EXER + CBT, exercise program plus support group sessions for lifestyle modification, with a CBT; and CONTROL, routine treatment. They were compared on weight, functional capacity, and cardiometabolic profile.

Results: The weight change (Kg) was -7.4 (-9.6 to 5,1); -4,2 (-6,8 to -1.6) and 2.9 (0.4 to 5.3) and the BMI change (kg/m2) was -2.7 (-3.6 to -1.8); -1.4 (-2.4 to -0.4) and 1.1 (0.1 to 2.1) for groups EXER, EXER + CBT, and CONTROL, respectively. Changes were significant when compared to the control group (p < 0.001), but there were no differences between the two intervention arms (p = 0.2). Functional capacity and cardiometabolic parameters significantly improved in the intervention arms and worsened in the control group. The adherence to the exercise program in both groups was above 78 %.

Conclusion: A 4-month, twice-weekly supervised program of low-intensity physical activity that encourages individuals to adopt a more active lifestyle can positively interfere with weight loss and improvement in functional capacity and cardiometabolic parameters of morbidly obese individuals with and without the aid of support group sessions.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine you have a bunch of really heavy people who are waiting for surgery to help them lose weight.  This study looked at how different ways of exercising and talking about healthy habits affected their weight and health.

They split the people into three groups:

* **Group 1 (EXER):**  Just did a light exercise program twice a week.
* **Group 2 (EXER + CBT):** Did the same light exercise program, plus extra talks and support sessions to help them change their eating and lifestyle habits.  These talks used a method called CBT (cognitive behavioral therapy).
* **Group 3 (CONTROL):**  Didn't do any special exercise program, just their normal routine.

They watched how much weight everyone lost, how well they could move, and how healthy their bodies were over four months.

The results showed that both exercise groups lost more weight and got healthier than the group that didn't do anything special.  The exercise group with the extra support sessions lost about the same amount of weight as the exercise group without the support sessions.  So, the extra support didn't make a big difference in weight loss.  But both exercise groups got better at moving and had healthier bodies than the group that didn't exercise.  Almost everyone in the exercise groups stuck with the program.

The scientists concluded that light exercise, even without extra support, is a good way to help heavy people lose weight and get healthier.

Title: What Is the Best Treatment before Bariatric Surgery? Exercise, Exercise and Group Therapy, or Conventional Waiting: a Randomized Controlled Trial.
abstract:
Objective: This trial's objective was to investigate the effect of an exercise program with and without cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT), compared by a control group, on weight of morbidly obese individuals while waiting for bariatric surgery.

Materials and methods: This randomized controlled trial investigated the effect of a 4-month low-intensity exercise program (two weekly sessions of 25 min each) on 66 morbidly obese individuals awaiting bariatric surgery. Participants were randomly divided into three groups: EXER, exercise program; EXER + CBT, exercise program plus support group sessions for lifestyle modification, with a CBT; and CONTROL, routine treatment. They were compared on weight.

Results: The weight change (Kg) was -7.4 (-9.6 to 5.1); -4.2 (-6.8 to -1.6) and 2.9 (0.4 to 5.3) for groups EXER, EXER + CBT, and CONTROL, respectively. Changes were significant when compared to the control group (p < 0.001), but there were no differences between the two intervention arms (p = 0.2). The adherence to the exercise program in both groups was above 78%.

Conclusion: The exercise program was not more effective than routine treatment in patients with morbid obesity awaiting bariatric surgery.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine you have a bunch of really heavy people who are going to get surgery to lose weight.  This study looked at whether a special exercise program, with or without extra help changing their habits (like CBT), would help them lose more weight while they wait for the surgery.

They split the people into three groups:

* **Group 1 (EXER):**  Just did the exercise program.
* **Group 2 (EXER + CBT):** Did the exercise program *and* got extra help changing their habits and lifestyle with CBT.
* **Group 3 (CONTROL):**  Didn't do any special program, just got their normal care.

After four months, they measured how much weight each group lost.

The exercise program *did* help people lose weight compared to the group that didn't do anything special.  But the extra CBT help didn't make them lose any more weight than just doing the exercise program alone.  So, the exercise program alone was enough, and adding CBT didn't make it better.  Most people in both exercise groups stuck with the program.

Title: A Prospective Randomized Clinical Trial in Total Hip Arthroplasty-Comparing Early Results Between the Direct Anterior Approach and the Posterior Approach.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim benefit based on subgroup analysis
abstract:
BACKGROUND: We report a prospective randomized study comparing early clinical results between the direct anterior approach (DAA) and posterior approach (PA) in primary hip arthroplasty.

METHODS: Surgeries were performed by 2 senior hip arthroplasty surgeons. Seventy-two patients with complete data were assessed preoperatively 2, 6, and 12 weeks postoperatively. The primary outcomes were the Western Ontario McMasters Arthritis Index and Oxford Hip Scores. Secondary outcome measures included the EuroQoL, 10-meter walk test, and clinical and radiographic parameters.

RESULTS: Data analyses showed no difference between DAA (nВ = 35) and PA (nВ = 37) groups when comparing total scores for primary outcomes. No significant differences were observed for 10-meter walk test, EuroQoL, and radiographic analyses. Subgroup analysis for surgeon 1 identified that the DAA group had shorter acute hospital stay, less postoperative opiate requirements, and smaller wounds. However, this was offset by increased operative time, higher intraoperative blood loss, and weaker hip flexion at 2 and 6 weeks. Subgroup analysis of items on the Western Ontario McMasters Arthritis Index and Oxford Hip Score identified that hip flexion activity favored the DAA group up to 6 weeks postoperatively. There was an 83% incidence of lateral cutaneous nerve of thigh neuropraxia at the 12-week mark in the DAA group. No neuropraxias occurred in the PA group. One dislocation occurred in each group. A single patient from the DAA group required reoperation for leg-length discrepancy.

CONCLUSION: DAA total hip arthroplasty (THA) has comparable results with PA THA. Choice of surgical approach for THA should be based on patient factors, surgeon preference, and experience.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine two different ways to fix a broken hip.  Doctors did a study to see which way was better.

They had two groups of people.  One group got a new hip using a "front" approach (DAA), and the other group got a new hip using a "back" approach (PA).  Both groups were treated by experienced doctors.

The doctors checked on the patients for a while after surgery, looking at things like how much pain they were in, how well their hip worked, how well they could walk, and how their new hip looked on X-rays.

The results?  Both ways worked about the same overall.  There wasn't a big difference in how well the patients felt or how their hips worked.

However, there were some small differences.  The "front" approach had a shorter hospital stay and less pain medicine, but it took longer to do the surgery, had more blood loss, and sometimes hurt a nerve in the leg.  The "back" approach didn't have any nerve problems.

So, the best way to fix a hip depends on the person and the doctor.  There's no single best way that works for everyone.

Title: A Prospective Randomized Clinical Trial in Total Hip Arthroplasty-Comparing Early Results Between the Direct Anterior Approach and the Posterior Approach.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim benefit based on subgroup analysis
abstract:
BACKGROUND: We report a prospective randomized study comparing early clinical results between the direct anterior approach (DAA) and posterior approach (PA) in primary hip arthroplasty.

METHODS: Surgeries were performed by 2 senior hip arthroplasty surgeons. Seventy-two patients with complete data were assessed preoperatively and at 2, 6, and 12 weeks postoperatively. The primary outcomes were the Western Ontario McMasters Arthritis Index and Oxford Hip Scores.

RESULTS: Data analyses showed no difference between DAA (n = 35) and PA (n = 37) groups when comparing total scores for primary outcomes. No significant differences were observed for the 10-meter walk test, EuroQoL, and radiographic analyses. There was an 83% incidence of lateral cutaneous nerve of thigh neuropraxia at the 12-week mark in the DAA group. No neuropraxias occurred in the PA group. One dislocation occurred in each group. CONCLUSION: The treatment DAA was not more effective than comparator PA in patients with primary hip arthroplasty.
plain_language_summary:
Doctors did a study to see if two different ways of replacing a hip joint were equally good.  They had two groups of people: one group got the "front" way (DAA) and the other group got the "back" way (PA).  Both groups were treated by experienced doctors.

The doctors checked how well the hips worked and how the patients felt for 12 weeks after surgery.  They used special tests to measure things like pain, how well the patients could walk, and how their hips looked on X-rays.

The results showed that both ways worked about the same.  There were no big differences in how well the hips worked, how the patients felt, or how the X-rays looked.  However, the "front" way (DAA) caused a nerve problem in the leg for about 83% of the people in that group.  The "back" way (PA) didn't cause that problem.  One person in each group had their hip come out of place (dislocate).  One person in the "front" group needed another surgery because their leg was the wrong length.

So, the doctors learned that the "back" way of replacing a hip was just as good as the "front" way, but with less risk of a nerve problem.

Title: Preemptive Analgesia in Hip Arthroscopy: A Randomized Controlled Trial of Preemptive Periacetabular or Intra-articular Bupivacaine in Addition to Postoperative Intra-articular Bupivacaine.
Journal: Arthroscopy: The Journal of Arthroscopic & Related Surgery
abstract:
Purpose: To evaluate and compare the efficacy of intra-articular and periacetabular blocks for postoperative pain control after hip arthroscopy.

Methods: Forty-two consecutive patients scheduled for hip arthroscopy were randomized into 2 postoperative pain control groups. One group received preemptive intra-articular 20 mL of bupivacaine 0.5% injection, and the second group received preemptive periacetabular 20 mL of bupivacaine 0.5% injection. Before closure all patients received an additional dose of 20 mL of bupivacaine 0.5% intra-articularly. Data were compared with respect to postoperative pain with visual analog scale (VAS) and analgesic consumption, documented in a pain diary for 2 weeks after surgery.

Results: Twenty-one patients were treated with intra-articular injection, and 21 patients with peri-acetabular injection. There were no significant differences with regards to patient demographics or surgical procedures. VAS scores recorded during the first 30 minutes postoperatively and 18 hours after surgery were significantly lower in the periacetabular group compared with in the intra-articular group (0.667 ± 1.49 vs 2.11 ± 2.29; P < .045 and 2.62 ± 2.2 vs 4.79 ± 2.6; P < .009). There were no differences between the groups with regard to analgesic consumption.

Conclusions: Periacetabular injection of bupivacaine 0.5% was superior to intra-articular injection in pain reduction after hip arthroscopy at 30 minutes and 18 hours postoperatively. However, total analgesic consumption over the first 2 postoperative weeks and VAS pain measurements were not significantly affected.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine doctors are trying to figure out the best way to make hip surgery less painful.  They took 42 people getting this surgery and split them into two groups.

One group got medicine injected *inside* their hip joint (intra-articular). The other group got the medicine injected *around* their hip socket (periacetabular).  Both groups got more medicine later.

They tracked how much pain the people felt using a pain scale (like a number line where 0 is no pain and 10 is the worst pain).  They also kept track of how much pain medicine each person needed.

The results showed that the people who got the medicine around their hip socket felt less pain right after surgery and 18 hours later.  But, surprisingly, both groups used about the same amount of pain medicine over the next two weeks.

So, the doctors learned that injecting medicine around the hip socket is better for short-term pain relief after hip surgery, but it doesn't seem to make a big difference in how much pain medicine people need overall.

Title: Preemptive Analgesia in Hip Arthroscopy: A Randomized Controlled Trial of Preemptive Periacetabular or Intra-articular Bupivacaine in Addition to Postoperative Intra-articular Bupivacaine.
Journal: Arthroscopy: The Journal of Arthroscopic & Related Surgery
abstract:
Purpose: To evaluate the efficacy of intra-articular versus periacetabular blocks for postoperative pain control after hip arthroscopy.

Methods: Forty-two consecutive patients scheduled for hip arthroscopy were randomized into two postoperative pain control groups. One group received preemptive intra-articular 20 mL of bupivacaine 0.5% injection, and the second group received preemptive periacetabular 20 mL of bupivacaine 0.5% injection. Before closure, all patients received an additional dose of 20 mL of bupivacaine 0.5% intra-articularly. The primary outcome was postoperative pain measured with a visual analog scale (VAS). Analgesic consumption was documented in a pain diary for 2 weeks after surgery.

Results: Twenty-one patients were treated with intra-articular injection, and 21 patients with periacetabular injection. There were no significant differences with regards to patient demographics or surgical procedures. VAS scores recorded during the first 30 minutes postoperatively and 18 hours after surgery were lower in the periacetabular group compared with the intra-articular group (0.667 ± 1.49 vs 2.11 ± 2.29; P < .045 and 2.62 ± 2.2 vs 4.79 ± 2.6; P < .009). There were no differences between the groups with regard to analgesic consumption.

Conclusions: The treatment with periacetabular injection was not more effective than intra-articular injection in patients with hip arthroscopy regarding overall pain control and analgesic consumption.
plain_language_summary:
Doctors wanted to see which way to numb a hip after surgery worked better.  They did surgery on 42 people and split them into two groups.  One group got medicine injected *inside* the hip joint (intra-articular), and the other group got medicine injected *around* the hip socket (periacetabular).  Both groups got more medicine later.  The doctors measured how much pain the people felt using a scale (like a smiley face scale).  They also tracked how much pain medicine the people needed.

The group that got the medicine around the hip socket had less pain right after surgery and 18 hours later.  But, both groups used about the same amount of pain medicine overall.  So, the doctors learned that injecting medicine around the hip socket didn't make pain control better than injecting it inside the hip joint.

Title: Laparoscopic Adjustable Gastric Banding: a Prospective Randomized Clinical Trial Comparing 5-Year Results of two Different Bands in 103 Patients.
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Various types of adjustable gastric bands are used during LAGB, but there is insufficient data comparing different bands in the long term. We carried out a prospective randomized study to compare two different bands.

METHODS: Between January 1, 2009 and January 31, 2010, 103 morbidly obese patients were randomized between SAGB and MiniMizer Extra adjustable gastric bands. The SAGB was used in 49 and MiniMizer Extra in 54 patients. Weight loss, comorbidities, long-term complications, and quality of life were evaluated after 5В years.

RESULTS: Patient baseline characteristics were similar in the two groups. The mean patient age was 45.9В В±В 11.7В years, and mean preoperative BMI was 47.5В В±В 7.3В kg/m2. A total of 90 of 103 patients (87.3В %) completed the 5-year follow-up. The mean excess weight loss was 44.1 and 50.3В % in SAGB and MiniMizer groups, respectively (pВ =В 0.14). A proportion of patients who reached a BMIВ <В 35В kg/m2 was significantly larger in MiniMizer Extra group (52.9 vs 25.5В %; pВ =В 0.01). Complications developed in 15 patients (14.5В %) and consisted of 5 band erosions, 4 port-related complications, 3 band slippages, and 3 band intolerances. All five band erosions developed in MiniMizer Extra group, but the difference was not significant (pВ =В 0.058). No difference was found regarding postoperative complications, resolution of comorbidities, and quality of life between compared groups.CONCLUSIONS: SAGB and MiniMizer Extra bands demonstrated similar long-term results regarding the weight loss, resolution of comorbidities, morbidity, and quality of life.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine two different types of special belts that doctors put around people's stomachs to help them lose weight.  Doctors wanted to see which belt worked better in the long run.

They picked 103 very overweight people and randomly put one type of belt on half of them and the other type on the other half.  They followed these people for 5 years, checking how much weight they lost, if their other health problems got better, and how their lives were.

Both types of belts helped people lose weight, but one type (MiniMizer) helped more people get their body mass index (BMI) down to a healthy level.  A few people had problems with both types of belts, but there wasn't a big difference between the two types of belts in how often problems happened.

Overall, both belts worked about the same in the long run for weight loss, helping people's other health problems, and improving their quality of life.

Title: Laparoscopic Adjustable Gastric Banding: a Prospective Randomized Clinical Trial Comparing 5-Year Results of two Different Bands in 103 Patients.
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Various types of adjustable gastric bands are used during LAGB, but there is insufficient data comparing different bands in the long term. We carried out a prospective randomized study to compare two different bands.

METHODS: Between January 1, 2009, and January 31, 2010, 103 morbidly obese patients were randomized between SAGB and MiniMizer Extra adjustable gastric bands. The SAGB was used in 49 and MiniMizer Extra in 54 patients. The primary outcome was weight loss evaluated after 5 years. Secondary outcomes included comorbidities, long-term complications, and quality of life.

RESULTS: Patient baseline characteristics were similar in the two groups. The mean patient age was 45.9 ± 11.7 years, and mean preoperative BMI was 47.5 ± 7.3 kg/m². A total of 90 of 103 patients (87.3%) completed the 5-year follow-up. The mean excess weight loss was 44.1% in the SAGB group and 50.3% in the MiniMizer group (p = 0.14). Complications developed in 15 patients (14.5%) and consisted of 5 band erosions, 4 port-related complications, 3 band slippages, and 3 band intolerances. All five band erosions developed in the MiniMizer Extra group, but the difference was not significant (p = 0.058). No difference was found regarding postoperative complications, resolution of comorbidities, and quality of life between the compared groups.CONCLUSIONS: The treatment with SAGB was not more effective than MiniMizer Extra in patients with morbid obesity regarding weight loss, resolution of comorbidities, morbidity, and quality of life.
plain_language_summary:
Doctors wanted to see which type of adjustable stomach band is better for helping very overweight people lose weight.  They picked two different types of bands, and randomly put 103 people into two groups.  One group got one type of band, and the other group got the other type.  They followed these people for 5 years to see how much weight they lost, if they had any problems, and how their health changed.

The results showed that both bands helped people lose a lot of weight, but there wasn't a big difference between the two.  About 87 out of 103 people finished the 5-year check-up.  One type of band seemed to cause more problems with the band itself, but the difference wasn't big enough to say one band was better than the other.  Overall, both bands helped people lose weight and improve their health, but there wasn't a clear winner between the two types.

Title: Management of benign paroxysmal positional vertigo: a randomized controlled trial.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim equivalence/non-inferiority versus control for a (-) endpoint
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Vertigo is a debilitating disease that is commonly encountered in the emergency department (ED). Diazepam and meclizine are oral medications that are commonly used to alleviate symptoms.

OBJECTIVES: We sought to determine whether meclizine or diazepam is superior in the treatment of patients with peripheral vertigo in the ED.

METHODS: We performed a double-blind clinical trial at a suburban, teaching ED. We randomized a convenience sample of adult patients with acute peripheral vertigo (APV) to diazepam 5 mg or meclizine 25 mg orally. Demographic and historical features were recorded on a standardized data form. Patients recorded their initial level (t0) of vertigo on a 100-mm visual analog scale (VAS) and after 30 min (t30) and 60 min (t60). The primary outcome parameter was the mean change in VAS score from t0 to t60. Differences between groups and 95% confidence intervals were calculated. Our a priori power calculation estimated that a sample size of 20 patients in each group was required to have an 80% power to detect a difference of 20 mm between treatment groups.

RESULTS: There were 20 patients in the diazepam group and 20 in the meclizine group. The two groups were similar with respect to patient demographics and presenting signs and symptoms. At t60, the mean improvements in the diazepam and meclizine groups were 36 and 40, respectively (difference -4; 95% confidence interval -20 to 12; p = 0.60).

CONCLUSION: The treatment with oral diazepam was not more effective than oral meclizine in patients with acute peripheral vertigo in the ED.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine you have a really bad spinning feeling in your head (vertigo).  Doctors wanted to see which medicine, diazepam or meclizine, works better to make that feeling go away faster.

They picked 40 people who had this spinning feeling and randomly gave half of them diazepam and half meclizine.  They tracked how much the spinning feeling improved for each person, using a special scale.

After an hour, they compared how much better each group felt.  They found that both medicines helped about the same amount.  So, neither medicine was clearly better than the other.

They measured how much the dizziness improved for each person after 30 minutes and 60 minutes.  They looked at 20 people who got each medicine. Title: Meal replacements followed by topiramate for the treatment of adolescent severe obesity: A pilot randomized controlled trial.
abstract:
OBJECTIVE: To assess the safety and efficacy of short-term meal replacement therapy followed by topiramate for body mass index (BMI) reduction in adolescents with severe obesity.

METHODS: Adolescents (ages 12-18 years) with severe obesity (BMI ≥1.2 times the 95th percentile or BMI ≥35 kg/m²) were recruited for this double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled trial. Participants completed 4 weeks of meal replacement therapy followed by randomization (1:1) to either 24 weeks of topiramate 75 mg/day or placebo. The primary outcome was the mean percent change in BMI between groups.

RESULTS: Thirty adolescents (mean age 15.2 ± 1.7 years, mean BMI 40.3 ± 4.6 kg/m²) completed the meal replacement phase and were randomized; 21 completed the study. The difference in mean percent change in BMI between the topiramate and placebo groups was not significant (-1.9%; 95% CI: -5.2% to +1.5%; P = 0.291). There were no concerning changes in neurocognitive function or bone health. CONCLUSIONS: The treatment with topiramate was not more effective than placebo in reducing BMI in adolescents with severe obesity.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine you have a really big problem, like being very overweight.  Doctors want to see if a special way of eating (meal replacement therapy) and a medicine called topiramate can help.

They took some kids who were very overweight (ages 12-18) and put them on a special diet for 4 weeks.  Then, they randomly split them into two groups.  One group took topiramate, and the other group took a fake medicine (placebo).  Both groups were kept secret about which medicine they were taking.

They watched how much the kids' weight changed over 24 weeks.  The doctors wanted to see if the topiramate group lost more weight than the placebo group.

Unfortunately, the topiramate group didn't lose significantly more weight than the placebo group.  The difference was very small, and the doctors weren't sure if the medicine helped.  They also checked if the medicine affected the kids' brains or bones, and it didn't seem to cause any problems.

So, the special diet and medicine together didn't seem to be a better way to lose weight than just the special diet alone.

Title: Meal replacements followed by topiramate for the treatment of adolescent severe obesity: A pilot randomized controlled trial.
abstract:
OBJECTIVE: To assess the safety and efficacy of short-term meal replacement therapy followed by topiramate for body mass index (BMI) reduction in adolescents with severe obesity.

METHODS: Adolescents (ages 12-18 years) with severe obesity (BMI в‰Ґ1.2 times the 95th percentile or BMI в‰Ґ35 kg/m2 ) were recruited for this double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled trial. Participants completed 4 weeks of meal replacement therapy followed by randomization (1:1) to either 24 weeks of topiramate 75 mg/day or placebo. Mean changes were compared between groups.

RESULTS: Thirty adolescents (mean age 15.2вЂ‰В±вЂ‰1.7 years, mean BMI 40.3вЂ‰В±вЂ‰4.6 kg/m2 ) completed the meal replacement phase and were randomized; 21 completed the study. The difference in mean percent change in BMI between the topiramate and placebo groups was not significant (-1.9%; 95% CI: -5.2% to +1.5%; PвЂ‰=вЂ‰0.291). Significant improvements in visceral fat and very-low-density lipoprotein cholesterol were observed in the topiramate compared with the placebo group. There were no concerning changes in neurocognitive function or bone health.CONCLUSIONS: In this pilot study, 4 weeks of meal replacement therapy followed by 24 weeks of low-dose topiramate compared with meal replacement therapy alone did not result in significant BMI reduction for adolescents with severe obesity.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine you have a really big problem, like being very overweight.  Doctors want to see if a special diet and a medicine can help.

This study looked at a group of teenagers who were very overweight.  They tried a special diet for a month, then some of them took a medicine called topiramate for another 6 months, while others took a fake medicine (placebo).

The goal was to see if the medicine helped them lose weight.  They measured how much weight they lost and looked at other things like fat around their organs and cholesterol.

The study found that the medicine didn't help them lose significantly more weight than just the special diet alone.  But, the medicine did seem to help with some other things, like reducing fat around their organs and improving a type of cholesterol.  It also didn't seem to hurt their brains or bones.

So, the special diet plus the medicine didn't make a big difference in weight loss, but it might have some other benefits.

Title: Comparison of Ramosetron With Ondansetron for Prevention of Intrathecal Morphine-Induced Nausea and Vomiting After Primary Total Knee Arthroplasty: A Randomized Control Trial.
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Postoperative nausea and vomiting (PONV) is a major concern following total knee arthroplasty (TKA) performed with spinal anesthesia and intrathecal morphine. 5-Hydroxytryptamine receptor 3 (5-HT3) antagonists like ondansetron and ramosetron are commonly used for preventing PONV, but the optimal choice remains unclear. We conducted a study to assess the effectiveness of ramosetron and ondansetron.

METHODS: Patients were randomized preoperatively to receive either intravenous ondansetron (8 mg) or ramosetron (0.3 mg). The primary outcome was the incidence rate of PONV. Secondary outcomes included the need for a rescue antiemetic (metoclopramide), pain score, patient satisfaction, and adverse drug reactions (ADRs) at 0-6, 6-12, 12-24, and 24-48 hours.

RESULTS: Ninety patients were evaluable, with 45 patients in each arm. The incidence rates of nausea were lower with ramosetron compared to ondansetron at 12-18 hours (3/45 vs 11/45, P = .016) and 18-24 hours (1/45 vs 9/45, P = .005). The incidence of vomiting was also lower with ramosetron at 18-24 hours (1/45 vs 6/45, P = .044). There were no significant differences between the groups in the use of rescue antiemetic medicine, pain score, patient satisfaction, and ADRs.

CONCLUSION: The treatment with ramosetron was not more effective than ondansetron in patients with PONV after TKA.
plain_language_summary:
Doctors sometimes give medicine to help people not get sick after surgery.  One kind of surgery is replacing a knee.  Sometimes, people get sick to their stomachs (nausea) or throw up (vomit) after this surgery.  This study looked at two medicines, ondansetron and ramosetron, to see which one was better at preventing this sickness.

They gave one medicine to some people and the other to other people, randomly.  They watched how many people got sick to their stomachs or threw up after the surgery, and how much medicine they needed to feel better.

The study found that ramosetron was slightly better at preventing nausea and vomiting in the first few days after surgery, especially during certain times.  But overall, there wasn't a big difference between the two medicines.  Both worked pretty much the same in helping people feel better after surgery.

Title: Comparison of Ramosetron With Ondansetron for Prevention of Intrathecal Morphine-Induced Nausea and Vomiting After Primary Total Knee Arthroplasty: A Randomized Control Trial.
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Spinal anesthesia with intrathecal morphine is a reliable, easy to apply, and cost effective method for controlling pain after total knee arthroplasty (TKA). However, postoperative nausea and vomiting (PONV) is a major concern. 5-Hydroxytryptamine receptor 3 (5-HT3) antagonists like ondansetron and ramosetron are commonly used for preventing PONV, but the optimal choice remains unclear. We conducted a study to assess the effectiveness of ramosetron and ondansetron, hypothesizing that the longer acting ramosetron may be better than ondansetron in the first 48В hours after TKA, performed with spinal anesthesia and intrathecal morphine.

METHODS: Patients were randomized preoperatively to either intravenous ondansetron (8 mg) or ramosetron (0.3 mg). The primary outcomes were the incidence rates of PONV. Secondary outcomes were the need for a rescue antiemetic (metoclopramide), pain score, patient satisfaction, and adverse drug reactions (ADRs) at 0-6, 6-12, 12-24, and 24-48В hours.

RESULTS: Ninety patients were evaluable; 45 patients/arm. Compared with ondansetron, ramosetron was associated with significantly lower incidence rates of nausea at: (1) 12-18В hours: 3/45 vs 11/45 (PВ = .016), (2)В 18-24В hours: 1/45 vs 9/45 (PВ = .005), and vomiting at 18-24В hours: 1/45 vs 6/45 (PВ = .044). There were no significant differences in the use of rescue-antiemetic medicine, pain score, patient satisfaction, and ADRs.

CONCLUSION: Preoperative ramosetron provided more prolonged PONV prophylaxis than ondansetron after TKA in our setting, and had a similar ADR profile. Other trials are needed to confirm our results before ramosetron is adopted widely.
plain_language_summary:
Doctors sometimes give spinal anesthesia and medicine to help with pain after knee surgery.  A problem is that some people get sick to their stomach (nausea and vomiting) afterward.  Doctors tried two different medicines to prevent this sickness: ondansetron and ramosetron.  One medicine (ramosetron) lasts longer than the other.

Researchers gave one medicine to some patients and the other to other patients.  They watched how many people got sick after surgery, and how long the sickness lasted.

The results showed that the longer-lasting medicine (ramosetron) worked better at preventing sickness for a longer time after surgery.  It helped fewer people get sick in the first two days.  The medicines didn't seem to cause other problems for the patients.

The researchers think ramosetron might be a good choice, but more studies are needed to be sure.

Title: Effect of early supervised physiotherapy on recovery from acute ankle sprain: randomised controlled trial
Spin in Abstract Title/Results: RESULTS: "Trend toward significance" // "Numerically longer survival" or similar verbiage
abstract:
Objective: To assess the efficacy of a programme of supervised physiotherapy on the recovery of simple grade 1 and 2 ankle sprains.

Design: A randomised controlled trial of 503 participants followed for six months.

Setting: Participants were recruited from two tertiary acute care settings in Kingston, ON, Canada.

Participants: The broad inclusion criteria were patients aged і16 presenting for acute medical assessment and treatment of a simple grade 1 or 2 ankle sprain. Exclusions were patients with multiple injuries, other conditions limiting mobility, and ankle injuries that required immobilisation and those unable to accommodate the time intensive study protocol.

Intervention: Participants received either usual care, consisting of written instructions regarding protection, rest, cryotherapy, compression, elevation, and graduated weight bearing activities, or usual care enhanced with a supervised programme of physiotherapy.

Main outcome measures: The primary outcome of efficacy was the proportion of participants reporting excellent recovery assessed with the foot and ankle outcome score (FAOS). Excellent recovery was defined as a score і450/500 at three months. A difference of at least 15% increase in the absolute proportion of participants with excellent recovery was deemed clinically important. Secondary analyses included the assessment of excellent recovery at one and six months; change from baseline using continuous scores at one, three, and six months; and clinical and biomechanical measures of ankle function, assessed at one, three, and six months.

Results: The absolute proportion of patients achieving excellent recovery at three months was not significantly different between the physiotherapy (98/229, 43%) and usual care (79/214, 37%) arms (absolute difference 6%, 95% confidence interval -3% to 15%). The observed trend towards benefit with physiotherapy did not increase in the per protocol analysis and was in the opposite direction by six months. These trends remained similar and were never statistically or clinically important when the FAOS was analysed as a continuous change score.

Conclusions: In a general population of patients seeking hospital based acute care for simple ankle sprains, there is no evidence to support a clinically important improvement in outcome with the addition of supervised physiotherapy to usual care, as provided in this protocol.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine you're playing basketball and you twist your ankle.  This study looked at how well a special program of physical therapy helps people recover from a mild ankle sprain.

They took 503 people who hurt their ankles at hospitals in Kingston, Canada.  Half of them got the usual care (like resting, icing, and elevating their ankle), and the other half got that usual care *plus* extra physical therapy sessions.

They followed everyone for six months, checking how well their ankles healed.  They used a special scorecard (the FAOS) to measure how good the recovery was.  A score of 450 or higher out of 500 meant a great recovery.

The results?  After three months, there wasn't much difference in how well the two groups healed.  While the physical therapy group *seemed* to do a little better, the difference wasn't big enough to be sure it was really helping.  By six months, the physical therapy group actually seemed to be doing *worse* than the usual care group.

So, the study found that for most people with mild ankle sprains, extra physical therapy didn't make a big difference in how quickly or well they recovered.

Title: Effect of early supervised physiotherapy on recovery from acute ankle sprain: randomised controlled trial

plain_language_summary:
Imagine you're trying to figure out the best way to heal a mild ankle sprain.  This study looked at two ways:

1. **Usual care:**  Following the usual advice – rest, ice, compression, elevation, and slowly starting to use your ankle again.

2. **Usual care plus physiotherapy:**  The usual care plus getting help from a physical therapist to do exercises and stretches.

They followed 503 people who had mild ankle sprains for six months.  They were all treated at hospitals in Kingston, Canada.

The researchers wanted to see if the physiotherapy helped people recover better.  They measured how well people's ankles healed by using a special score (FAOS).  A score of 450 or higher out of 500 meant a very good recovery.

The results showed that the physiotherapy group and the usual care group both did pretty much the same.  There wasn't a big difference in how well people recovered.  So, for mild ankle sprains, getting physiotherapy probably isn't much better than just following the usual advice.

Title: Efficacy of a Single Dose and an Additional Dose of Tranexamic Acid in Reduction of Blood Loss in Total Knee Arthroplasty.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Other
abstract:
Abstract: Background: This study aimed to evaluate the efficacy and safety of different regimens of tranexamic acid (TA) in reducing blood loss in total knee arthroplasty (TKA).

Methods: A total of 180 patients scheduled for primary unilateral TKA were randomly assigned to groups A, B, C, and D. Preoperative dose, an additional postoperative dose, and two postoperative additional doses of TA were infused intravenously in groups A (30 mg/kg), B (15 mg/kg), and C (10 mg/kg), respectively, while TA was not infused in group D. The primary outcome was total blood loss. Secondary outcomes included blood transfusion rate and volume, hemoglobin level, and incidence of deep vein thrombosis.

Results: The total blood loss was lower in groups B and C compared to group D. There was no significant difference in intraoperative blood loss between groups A and B and between groups B and C. Safety data showed no significant differences in the incidence of deep vein thrombosis among the groups.

Conclusion: The treatment with tranexamic acid was not more effective than no treatment in reducing total blood loss in patients undergoing total knee arthroplasty.
plain_language_summary:
This study looked at how different amounts of a medicine called tranexamic acid (TA) affect blood loss during knee replacement surgery.

They divided people getting knee replacements into four groups.  Three groups got different amounts of TA before and after surgery, and one group got no TA.

The researchers measured how much blood each person lost during and after surgery, how many needed blood transfusions, and if they got blood clots.

The results showed that giving some amounts of TA did help reduce blood loss compared to not giving any TA.  But, the different amounts of TA didn't make a big difference in how much blood was lost.  Also, the medicine seemed safe, as it didn't cause more blood clots.

Basically, the study found that giving TA didn't make the surgery significantly better in terms of blood loss compared to not giving it at all.

Title: Efficacy of a Single Dose and an Additional Dose of Tranexamic Acid in Reduction of Blood Loss in Total Knee Arthroplasty.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Other
abstract:
Background: This study aimed at evaluating the efficacy and safety of different regimens with a single dose and an additional postoperative dose of tranexamic acid (TA) in reducing blood loss in total knee arthroplasty (TKA).

Methods: A total of 180 patients who were scheduled to undergo primary unilateral TKA in our level I trauma center were recorded and divided into groups A, B, C, and D randomly. Preoperative dose, an additional postoperative dose, and 2 postoperative additional doses of TA were infused intravenously in groups A (30 mg/kg), B (15 mg/kg), and C (10 mg/kg), respectively, and TA was not infused in group D. Blood loss (intraoperative, postoperative, and total blood loss), blood transfusion rate and volume, hemoglobin level, and incidence of deep vein thrombosis were analyzed.

Results: The total blood loss and postoperative blood loss were significantly lower in groups B and C without any difference between them. As for intraoperative blood loss, there was no significant difference between groups A and B and between groups B and C with superior efficacy than group D.

Conclusion: With the same total dose of TA, the preoperative dose and an additional dose of TA were superior to a single preoperative dose of TA in reducing blood loss in TKA. Besides, 1 additional dose was comparable to 2 additional doses of TA in reducing blood loss.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine doctors are trying to figure out the best way to stop bleeding during knee replacement surgery.  They took 180 people getting their knees replaced and split them into four groups.

* **Group A:** Got a big dose of a medicine (tranexamic acid) before surgery, and another dose after.
* **Group B:** Got a medium dose of the medicine before surgery, and another dose after.
* **Group C:** Got a small dose of the medicine before surgery, and another dose after.
* **Group D:** Didn't get any of the medicine.

The doctors measured how much blood each person lost during and after surgery, how many blood transfusions they needed, and if they got blood clots.

The results showed that the groups that got *some* medicine before and after surgery lost less blood overall and after surgery, compared to the group that didn't get any medicine.  The groups that got a medium or small dose of the medicine before and after surgery lost about the same amount of blood.  The group that got the biggest dose before and after surgery didn't do much better than the group that got a medium dose.

So, the best way to reduce blood loss during knee replacement surgery seems to be giving a dose of the medicine before surgery, and another dose after.  Giving more than one extra dose after surgery didn't seem to help much more than just one extra dose.

Title: Intranasal Lidocaine in Acute Treatment of Migraine: A Randomized Controlled Trial.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Focus on another objective (e.g., trial is (-) but they say they accomplish some goal that they did not prespecify)
abstract:
Context: The study aims to evaluate the efficacy and safety of intranasal lidocaine administration for migraine treatment.

Methods: This single-center, double-blind, randomized, controlled trial was conducted in a tertiary care emergency department. Included patients met the migraine criteria of the International Headache Society. Patients were randomized to intranasal lidocaine or saline solution; all participants received 10 mg of intravenous metoclopramide. The primary outcome measure was the change in pain scores at 15 minutes.

Results: Patients (n=162) were randomized into 2 groups with similar baseline migraine characteristics and numeric rating scale scores. The median reduction in numeric rating scale score at 15 minutes was 3 (interquartile range [IQR] 2 to 5) for the lidocaine group and 2 (IQR 1 to 4) for the saline solution group (median difference=1.0; 95% confidence interval 0.1 to 2.1). Need for rescue medication did not differ between the groups, and local irritation was the most common adverse event in the lidocaine group.

Conclusion: The treatment with intranasal lidocaine was not more effective than saline solution in patients with migraine regarding the primary outcome of change in pain scores at 15 minutes.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine doctors are testing a new way to treat headaches (migraines).  They picked a bunch of people who had migraines and split them into two groups.

One group got a special nose spray with medicine (lidocaine). The other group got a nose spray with just salt water (saline).  Both groups also got a medicine through a vein (IV).

The doctors measured how much the headaches hurt before and 15 minutes after the nose spray.

The group that got the medicine spray had their headaches get a little better (about 3 points on a pain scale). The group that got the salt water spray had their headaches get a little better too (about 2 points).

The difference wasn't very big.  The medicine spray didn't make headaches go away any better than the salt water spray.  The medicine spray did cause some nose irritation in some people.

So, the nose spray medicine didn't seem to work better than a salt water spray for treating migraines in this study.

Title: Intranasal Lidocaine in Acute Treatment of Migraine: A Randomized Controlled Trial.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Focus on another objective (e.g., trial is (-) but they say they accomplish some goal that they did not prespecify)
abstract:
Study objective: The study aims to evaluate the efficacy and safety of intranasal lidocaine administration for migraine treatment.

Methods: This single-center, double-blind, randomized, controlled trial was conducted in a tertiary care emergency department. Included patients met the migraine criteria of the International Headache Society. Patients were randomized to intranasal lidocaine or saline solution; all participants received 10 mg of intravenous metoclopramide. Patient pain intensity was assessed with an 11-point numeric rating scale score. The primary outcome measure was the change in pain scores at 15 minutes; secondary outcomes were changes in pain intensity after pain onset and need for rescue medication.

Results: Patients (n=162) were randomized into 2 groups with similar baseline migraine characteristics and numeric rating scale scores. The median reduction in numeric rating scale score at 15 minutes was 3 (interquartile range [IQR] 2 to 5) for the lidocaine group and 2 (IQR 1 to 4) for the saline solution group (median difference=1.0; 95% confidence interval 0.1 to 2.1). The reduction in pain score at 30 minutes was 4 (IQR 3 to 7) for the lidocaine group and 5 (IQR 2 to 7) for the saline solution group (median difference=1.0; 95% confidence interval 0.1 to 2.1). Need for rescue medication did not differ between the groups, and local irritation was the most common adverse event in the lidocaine group.

Conclusion: Although intranasal lidocaine was found no more efficacious than normal saline solution in our study, future studies should focus on patients who present earlier after headache onset.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine doctors are trying to figure out if a special nose spray can help people with bad headaches (migraines).

They picked a bunch of people who had migraines and split them into two groups.  One group got the nose spray with lidocaine, and the other group got a harmless salt water spray.  Both groups also got a medicine to help their stomachs.

The doctors measured how much the headaches hurt using a scale of 0 to 10.  They checked the pain 15 minutes and 30 minutes after the spray.

The results showed that the nose spray with lidocaine helped a little bit more than the salt water spray, but not a lot.  Both sprays helped reduce pain, but the difference wasn't big enough to be sure.

The nose spray sometimes made people's noses a little sore, but that wasn't a big problem.

The doctors think that maybe if they tested the nose spray on people who get headaches right away, it might work better.

Title: Comparison of intranasal ketamine versus IV morphine in reducing pain in patients with renal colic.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim benefit based on (+) subgroup analysis
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Various drugs have been used to relieve abdominal pain in patients with renal colic. Ketamine is a popular choice as an analgesic.OBJECTIVE: To compare the effectiveness of intranasal (IN) ketamine versus intravenous (IV) morphine in reducing pain in patients with renal colic.

METHODS: A randomized double-blind controlled trial was performed in 53 patients with renal colic recruited from the emergency department (ED) in 2015. Finally, 40 patients were enrolled in this study. Patients in the ketamine group received IN ketamine 1 mg/kg and IV placebo while patients in the control group received IV morphine 0.1mg/kg and IN placebo. Our goal was to assess visual analogue scale (VAS) changes between the 2 groups. Patients' VAS scores were reported before and 5, 15, 30min after drug injection.

RESULTS: Before drug administration, the mean±SD VAS score was 7.40±1.18 in the morphine group (group A) and 8.35±1.30 in the ketamine group (group B) (P-value=0.021). After adjustment by the appropriate analysis, the mean±SD VAS score in group (A) and (B) at 5min were (6.07±0.47 vs 6.87±0.47; mean difference -0.79, 95% confidence interval (CI) -1.48 to -1.04) (P-value=0.025), at 15 and 30min, the mean±SD VAS score in group (A) and (B) were (5.24±0.49 vs 5.60±0.49; mean difference -0.36, 95% CI -1.08 to 0.34) and (4.02±0.59 vs 4.17±0.59; mean difference -0.15, 95% CI -1.02 to 0.71) (P-value=0.304 and 0.719) respectively.CONCLUSIONS: The treatment with IN ketamine was not more effective than IV morphine in patients with renal colic.
