The change in BMI SDS at 12 months from baseline was -0.12 in the control group and -0.10 in the intervention group. Improvements in cardiovascular fitness and quality of life were observed. Safety data, including reasons for withdrawals and treatment discontinuation, were collected.CONCLUSIONS: The treatment was not more effective than the comparator in patients with a change in BMI SDS.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine a group of kids who are overweight.  Scientists wanted to see if a special program could help them lose weight.

They divided the kids into two groups.  One group got a lot of help, with special meetings and checkups at home every six months, and a whole year of weekly group sessions. The other group got less help, just checkups and advice every six months.

The scientists tracked how much the kids' weight changed over a year.  They looked at how their heart health and happiness improved, too.

The results showed that the special program didn't make a bigger difference in weight loss than the less intense program.  Both groups had some improvement, but not a huge amount.

Title: Ondansetron treatment reduces rotavirus symptoms-A randomized double-blinded placebo-controlled trial.
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Rotavirus and norovirus cause acute gastroenteritis with severe diarrhea and vomiting, symptoms that may lead to severe dehydration and death. The objective of this randomized double-blinded placebo-controlled study was to investigate whether ondansetron, a serotonin receptor antagonist, could attenuate rotavirus- and norovirus-induced vomiting and diarrhea.

METHODS: Children with acute gastroenteritis, aged 6 months to 16 years, were enrolled (n = 104) and randomized to one single oral dose (0.15mg/kg) of ondansetron (n = 52) or placebo (n = 52). The primary outcome was the number of diarrhea and vomiting episodes during the 24 hours following treatment. Pathogens in feces were diagnosed by real-time PCR.

RESULTS: One dose of oral ondansetron reduced the duration of rotavirus clinical symptoms, with a median of two days. The number of diarrhea episodes was also reported. Safety data, including reasons for withdrawals and treatment discontinuation, were collected.

CONCLUSION: The treatment with ondansetron was not more effective than placebo in patients with acute gastroenteritis in reducing the number of diarrhea and vomiting episodes.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine you're sick with a stomach bug, like the flu, but really bad.  Doctors want to know if a medicine called ondansetron can help.

They took a bunch of kids who were sick with this stomach bug (rotavirus and norovirus) and gave half of them ondansetron and half of them a sugar pill (placebo).  They watched how many times the kids threw up and had diarrhea for 24 hours after taking the medicine.

The medicine didn't make the kids sick any less.  It didn't stop the throwing up and diarrhea any better than the sugar pill.  So, the medicine didn't really help.  It did seem to shorten the time the rotavirus symptoms lasted, but not the norovirus symptoms.

Title: Ondansetron treatment reduces rotavirus symptoms-A randomized double-blinded placebo-controlled trial.
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Rotavirus and norovirus cause acute gastroenteritis with severe diarrhoea and vomiting, symptoms that may lead to severe dehydration and death. The objective of this randomized double-blinded placebo-controlled study was to investigate whether ondansetron, a serotonin receptor antagonist could attenuate rotavirus- and norovirus-induced vomiting and diarrhoea, which would facilitate oral rehydration and possibly accelerate recovery and reduce need for hospitalization.

METHODS: Children with acute gastroenteritis, aged 6 months to 16 years where enrolled (n = 104) and randomized to one single oral dose (0.15mg/kg) of ondansetron (n = 52) or placebo (n = 52). The number of diarrhoea and vomiting episodes during the 24 hours following treatment was reported as well as the number of days with symptoms. Pathogens in faeces were diagnosed by real-time PCR. Outcome parameters were analyzed for rotavirus- and norovirus-positive children.

RESULTS: One dose of oral ondansetron reduced duration of rotavirus clinical symptoms (p = 0.014), with a median of two days. Furthermore, ondansetron reduced diarrhea episodes, most pronounced in children that had been sick for more than 3 days before treatment (p = 0.028).

CONCLUSION: Ondansetron may be a beneficial treatment for children with rotavirus gastroenteritis.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine you have a really bad stomach bug, like the kind that makes you throw up and have diarrhea.  This study looked at a medicine called ondansetron to see if it could help kids get better faster.

Scientists took a bunch of kids with these stomach bugs and randomly gave some of them ondansetron and others a sugar pill (placebo).  They watched how many times the kids threw up and had diarrhea, and how long their symptoms lasted.

They found that the kids who took ondansetron had their symptoms last shorter, especially if they had been sick for a while.  The medicine seemed to help reduce the number of times they threw up and had diarrhea.

So, the study shows that ondansetron might be a good treatment for kids with stomach bugs caused by rotavirus, a common virus that causes these problems.

Title: A Randomized, Controlled, Multicenter Study of Technology-Based Weight Loss Interventions among Endometrial Cancer Survivors.
Spin in Abstract Title/Results: RESULTS: Focus on (+) secondary endpoint; RESULTS: Other
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Focus on another objective (e.g., trial is (-) but they say they accomplish some goal that they did not prespecify)
abstract:
OBJECTIVE: The aim of this study was to test the efficacy of technology-based weight loss interventions for endometrial cancer (EC) survivors with obesity.

METHODS: EC survivors with obesity (n = 196) from three medical centers were randomized to a 6-month intervention: telemedicine with Wi-Fi scales, text messaging (texting), or enhanced usual care (EUC). The primary outcome was weight loss. Changes in anthropometrics and psychosocial measures were analyzed.

RESULTS: There were no significant differences in weight loss across interventions (mean = -4.4 kg, SD = 6.5 kg). Safety data, including reasons for withdrawals and treatment discontinuation, were not reported.CONCLUSIONS: The treatment interventions were not more effective than the comparator in patients with endometrial cancer survivors with obesity.
plain_language_summary:
This study looked at ways to help people who have had endometrial cancer and are overweight lose weight.  They tried using a special computer program, scales that connect to the internet, and text messages.  They followed 196 people for six months.

The study found that none of the methods were better than just getting regular medical care.  Everyone lost an average of about 4.4 kilograms (almost 10 pounds), but there was a lot of variation in how much people lost.  The study didn't say if any of the methods were harmful.

Title: A Randomized, Controlled, Multicenter Study of Technology-Based Weight Loss Interventions among Endometrial Cancer Survivors.
Spin in Abstract Title/Results: RESULTS: Focus on (+) secondary endpoint; RESULTS: Other
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Focus on another objective (e.g., trial is (-) but they say they accomplish some goal that they did not prespecify)
abstract:
OBJECTIVE: The aim of this study was to test the efficacy of technology-based weight loss interventions for endometrial cancer (EC) survivors with obesity.

METHODS: EC survivors with obesity (nвЂ‰=вЂ‰196) from three medical centers completed assessments for knowledge of obesity as a risk for EC and interest in weight management. Forty-one women were randomized to a 6-month intervention: telemedicine with Wi-Fi scales, text messaging (texting), or enhanced usual care (EUC). Changes in anthropometrics and psychosocial measures were analyzed.

RESULTS: One-third of survey participants lacked awareness that obesity increased the risk of EC, and 40% misclassified their body mass. There were no significant differences in weight loss across interventions (meanвЂ‰=вЂ‰-4.4 kg, SDвЂ‰=вЂ‰6.5 kg). Telemedicine showed improvements in physical health and cancer-related body image (PsвЂ‰=вЂ‰0.04) compared to texting and in sexual functioning compared to EUC (PвЂ‰=вЂ‰0.03). Total physical activity was increased in EUC compared with telemedicine (PвЂ‰=вЂ‰0.01), and vigorous physical activity was increased in EUC compared with both interventions (PвЂ‰=вЂ‰0.01-0.03); walking significantly increased in texting compared with telemedicine (PвЂ‰=вЂ‰0.02).CONCLUSIONS: Technology-based lifestyle interventions in EC survivors with obesity were accessible and resulted in weight loss and improved quality of life. EUC also produced weight loss, demonstrating a potential for beginning weight management with information on specific diet and exercise goals.
plain_language_summary:
This study looked at how technology can help women who have had endometrial cancer (a type of cancer in the uterus) and are overweight lose weight.

They had 196 women from three hospitals take part.  Some of the women got help losing weight using a special computer program (telemedicine) and scales that sent their weight to the computer. Others got help through text messages, and some got regular medical care (the "usual care").

The study found that many of the women didn't know that being overweight could make endometrial cancer worse, and some didn't know how much they weighed.  The study also found that all the groups lost some weight, but there wasn't a big difference in how much weight each group lost.

The telemedicine group had better results in some areas, like feeling better about their bodies and their sex lives.  The group that got regular medical care (usual care) actually got more exercise, but the telemedicine group did better in other areas.  The text message group did better at walking.

Overall, the study showed that using technology to help women lose weight is a good way to help them feel better and lose weight, and even regular medical care can help with weight loss.

Title: Behavioral Weight Loss Intervention for Migraine: A Randomized Controlled Trial.
Spin in Abstract Title/Results: Other - distract from negative trial by claiming need for future research
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Other
abstract:
Objective: The objective of this study was to test whether behavioral weight loss (BWL) intervention decreases headaches in women with comorbid migraine and overweight or obesity.

Methods: This randomized, single-blind trial allocated women 18 to 50 years old with 4 to 20 migraine days per month and a BMI = 25.0-49.9 kg/m2 to 16 weeks of BWL (n = 54), which targeted exercise and eating behaviors for weight loss, or to migraine education control (ME, n = 56), which delivered didactic instruction on migraine and treatments. Participants completed a 4-week smartphone headache diary at baseline, posttreatment (16-20 wk), and follow-up (32-36 wk). The primary outcome was posttreatment change in migraine days per month, analyzed via linear mixed effects models.

Results: Of 110 participants randomly assigned, 85 (78%) and 80 (73%) completed posttreatment and follow-up. Although the BWL group achieved greater weight loss (mean [95% CI] in kilograms) than the ME group at posttreatment (-3.8 [-2.5 to -5.0] vs. + 0.9 [-0.4 to 2.2], P < 0.001) and follow-up (-3.2 [-2.0 to -4.5] vs. + 1.1 [-0.2 to 2.4], P < 0.001), there were no significant group (BWL vs. ME) differences (mean [95% CI]) in migraine days per month at posttreatment (-3.0 [-2.0 to -4.0] vs. -4.0 [-2.9 to -5.0], P = 0.185) or follow-up (-3.8 [-2.7 to -4.8] vs. -4.4 [-3.4 to -5.5], P = 0.378).

Conclusions: Contrary to hypotheses, BWL and ME yielded similar, sustained reductions in migraine headaches. Future research should evaluate whether adding BWL to standard pharmacological and/or nonpharmacological migraine treatment approaches yields greater benefits.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine you have a group of women who have migraines and are overweight.  Scientists wanted to see if losing weight would help reduce their migraines.

They split the women into two groups.  One group followed a program to lose weight by changing their eating and exercise habits (the "weight loss" group). The other group learned about migraines and how to treat them (the "education" group).

Both groups tracked their migraines using a phone app.  The scientists measured how many migraine days each group had before, after 16 weeks, and again after another 16 weeks.

The weight loss group lost significantly more weight than the education group.  However, both groups had about the same number of migraine days after the 16 weeks, and this difference didn't change much after the second 16 weeks.

So, the study found that losing weight didn't seem to help reduce migraines more than just learning about them.  The scientists think more research is needed to see if losing weight *in addition* to other migraine treatments might be helpful.

plain_language_summary:
Imagine some women who have both migraines and are overweight.  Scientists wanted to see if losing weight through exercise and healthy eating (called "behavioral weight loss" or BWL) could help reduce their migraines.

They split the women into two groups. One group got help with losing weight, and the other group learned about migraines and how to manage them (the "migraine education" or ME group).  Both groups kept a diary of their headaches using a smartphone.

After 16 weeks, the scientists looked at how many headache days each group had.  Surprisingly, both groups had about the same number of headaches, whether they lost weight or just learned about migraines.  So, losing weight didn't seem to help reduce migraines more than learning about them.

Title: Platelet-Rich Plasma Intra-articular Knee Injections Show No Superiority Versus Viscosupplementation: A Randomized Controlled Trial.
Journal: The American Journal of Sports Medicine
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Platelet-rich plasma (PRP) injections have been proposed as a new conservative option for knee degeneration to provide symptomatic relief and delay surgical intervention. Although the current literature provides some evidence on the benefits of this technique compared with viscosupplementation, no studies have been performed to compare their long-term effects.

PURPOSE: To compare the long-term clinical outcomes provided by intra-articular injections of either PRP or hyaluronic acid (HA) to treat knee degenerative disease.

STUDY DESIGN: Randomized controlled trial; Level of evidence, 1.

METHODS: Patients with a history of chronic symptomatic knee degenerative changes and osteoarthritis (Kellgren-Lawrence grade 0-3) were enrolled: 192 patients were randomized to undergo 3 blinded weekly intra-articular injections of either PRP or HA. Patients were prospectively evaluated before the injection and then at 2, 6, 12, and 24 months and a mean of 64.3 months (SD, 7.8 months) of follow-up. Evaluation was based on International Knee Documentation Committee (IKDC) subjective (main outcome), EuroQol visual analog scale, and Tegner scores; 167 patients reached the final evaluation.

RESULTS: Both treatments were effective in improving knee functional status and symptoms over time: Mean В± SD IKDC subjective score improved significantly for both PRP and HA groups ( P < .0005) and remained stable over time up to 24 months (from 53.3 В± 14.3 to 67.3 В± 18.1 and from 50.3 В± 13.2 to 62.1 В± 20.8 for PRP and HA groups, respectively). At final evaluation, a significant IKDC reduction was observed in both treatment groups, with the PRP group still presenting significantly higher values compared with baseline: PRP 60.5 В± 19.0 ( P < .001 vs baseline), HA 55.7 В± 18.8 (not significant vs baseline). A comparative analysis showed no significant intergroup difference in any of the clinical scores at any follow-up point. The median duration of patient subjective perception of symptomatic relief was 9 months for HA and 12 months for PRP (not significant). The only significant difference was observed in the rate of reintervention at 24 months, which was significantly lower in the PRP group (22.6% vs 37.1%, P = .036).

CONCLUSION: Both treatments were effective in improving knee functional status and symptoms over time. PRP did not provide an overall superior clinical improvement compared with HA in terms of either symptomatic-functional improvement at different follow-up points or effect duration.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine you have a sore knee.  Doctors sometimes use shots to help.  This study looked at two different kinds of shots:  one with a special blood part called PRP, and one with a substance called HA.

They gave these shots to lots of people with sore knees.  They checked how the people felt and how well their knees worked over a long time (almost 5 years!).

The good news:  Both shots helped the people's knees feel better and work better.  The scores showed improvement for both groups.

The not-so-good news:  One shot (PRP) seemed to help people's knees stay better longer, and it seemed to help prevent needing another treatment more often.  But overall, there wasn't a big difference between the two shots in how much they helped.  Both shots worked pretty well.

plain_language_summary:
Imagine your knee is like a worn-out toy.  Doctors are trying different ways to fix it without surgery.  They tested two options:  PRP (like a special blood treatment) and HA (a type of gel).

They took a bunch of people with worn-out knees and randomly put them into two groups.  One group got PRP injections, and the other got HA injections.  They checked how the knees felt every few months for a long time (almost 5 years!).

The results?  Both treatments helped the knees feel better at first, but after a while, there wasn't much difference between the two.  While both groups felt better, the PRP group had slightly better results and needed less extra treatment later on.  The PRP group's knees felt better for a little longer, about 12 months on average, compared to the HA group's 9 months.

So, the study showed that PRP wasn't better than HA for fixing worn-out knees in the long run.

Title: The use of exparel (liposomal bupivacaine) to manage postoperative pain in unilateral total knee arthroplasty patients.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim equivalence/non-inferiority versus control for a (-) endpoint
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Local periarticular infiltration (PAI) analgesia has emerged as an important component of multimodal approaches to treat total knee arthroplasty postoperative pain. Liposomal bupivacaine may provide prolonged analgesic duration when injected into the surrounding tissues. The purpose of this study was to compare the analgesic efficacy and serum bupivacaine levels of a continuous femoral nerve block (CFNB) with bupivacaine to PAI with liposomal bupivacaine.

METHODS: Sixty-five patients undergoing primary unilateral total knee arthroplasty were randomized into 2 groups: (1) CFNB and PAI with bupivacaine (CFNB group) or (2) PAI with bupivacaine:liposomal bupivacaine mixture at the end of surgery (LB group). The primary outcome was pain intensity at maximum knee flexion 24В hours following surgery. Secondary outcomes included pain intensities at rest and movement at timed intervals and serum bupivacaine levels.

RESULTS: Patients in the CFNB group experienced lower pain scores at maximum knee flexion at 24В hours (7.91; 95% confidence interval, 7.19-8.61) compared to the LB group (8.95; 95% confidence interval, 8.42-9.48; PВ = .02). The mean peak serum bupivacaine level in the LB group up to 72В hours was 0.55 Ојg/mL versus 1.4 Ојg/mL for CFNB group (PВ = .0008) with one patient in the CFNB group exceeding the reported minimum serum bupivacaine threshold for toxicity.

CONCLUSION: While similar pain control was observed on the day of surgery for both groups, patients with a CFNB experienced lower pain intensities during maximum knee flexion at 24В hours. Total serum concentrations in LB group remained below the toxicity threshold over the study period.
plain_language_summary:
Doctors are trying to figure out the best way to help people who have knee surgery feel less pain.  They did a test with two different ways to numb the area around the knee.

One group got a continuous nerve block (like a special numbing medicine that keeps flowing into the nerve). The other group got a different numbing medicine that was mixed with a special kind of fat bubble (liposomal).

They measured how much pain each group felt 24 hours after surgery, and how much of the numbing medicine was in their blood.

The group that got the continuous nerve block had less pain 24 hours later.  The group that got the special fat bubble medicine had lower levels of the numbing medicine in their blood, and no one in that group had too much medicine in their blood, which could be dangerous.

So, the continuous nerve block seemed to be better at controlling pain 24 hours after surgery, but both methods worked well on the day of surgery.

Title: The use of exparel (liposomal bupivacaine) to manage postoperative pain in unilateral total knee arthroplasty patients.

plain_language_summary:
Imagine you're getting a new knee.  Doctors use different ways to help with the pain after surgery.  This study looked at two ways:

1. **Continuous Femoral Nerve Block (CFNB):**  This is like a special injection that numbs a nerve that sends pain signals to your brain.

2. **Periarticular Infiltration (PAI) with liposomal bupivacaine:** This is a shot of medicine around the knee joint to numb the area.  The "liposomal" part means the medicine is wrapped in a special coating that helps it last longer.

The study looked at which method worked better at reducing pain 24 hours after surgery.  They gave one group the CFNB and the other group the PAI with liposomal bupivacaine.

The results showed that the PAI with liposomal bupivacaine wasn't *worse* than the CFNB, but it didn't work *better* either.  The pain was about the same.  The liposomal bupivacaine also stayed in the blood at lower levels for longer, which is good because it means less chance of too much medicine in the body.  One person in the CFNB group had a level of medicine that was close to being too much.

So, for pain relief after knee surgery, both methods are about the same.

Title: Platelet-rich plasma for arthroscopic repair of medium to large rotator cuff tears: a randomized controlled trial.
Journal: The American Journal of Sports Medicine
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim benefit based on (+) secondary endpoint
abstract:
BACKGROUND: It is unclear whether leukocyte-poor (LP) or leukocyte-rich (LR) varieties of platelet-rich plasma (PRP) as an adjuvant to arthroscopic rotator cuff repair (ARCR) result in improved tendon healing rates.

PURPOSE: To perform a network meta-analysis of the randomized controlled trials in the literature to ascertain whether there is evidence to support the use of LP- or LR-PRP as an adjunct to ARCR.

METHODS: The literature search was based on the PRISMA (Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses) guidelines. Randomized controlled trials comparing LP- or LR-PRP with a control alongside ARCR were included. The primary outcome was the rate of retear and/or incomplete tendon healing after fixation. Clinical outcomes were compared using a frequentist approach to network meta-analysis, with statistical analysis performed using R. The treatment options were ranked using the P-score.

RESULTS: There were 13 studies (868 patients) included, with 9 studies comparing LP-PRP with a control and 4 studies comparing LR-PRP with a control. LP-PRP was found to reduce the rate of retear and/or incomplete tendon healing after fixation. LP-PRP had the highest P-score for all treatment groups. LR-PRP did not result in significant improvements over the control group. Post hoc analysis revealed that LP-PRP did not lead to significant improvements over LR-PRP in any category.

CONCLUSION: The treatment LP-PRP was not more effective than LR-PRP in patients with arthroscopic rotator cuff repair on the rate of retear and/or incomplete tendon healing.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine you're trying to fix a torn muscle in your arm.  Doctors sometimes use a special liquid called platelet-rich plasma (PRP) to help it heal.  There are two types of PRP: one with lots of white blood cells (leukocyte-rich) and one with fewer white blood cells (leukocyte-poor).  

This study looked at which type of PRP is better for fixing torn rotator cuff muscles during surgery.  They looked at lots of studies of people who had this surgery and used one of the two types of PRP.

The results showed that the PRP with fewer white blood cells (leukocyte-poor) seemed to help the muscle heal better, reducing the chance of it tearing again or not healing completely.  However, the PRP with more white blood cells (leukocyte-rich) didn't seem to help much compared to just having the surgery without PRP.  And importantly, the PRP with fewer white blood cells wasn't *significantly* better than the PRP with more white blood cells.  So, for this type of surgery, the type of PRP doesn't really matter much.

Title: Platelet-rich plasma for arthroscopic repair of medium to large rotator cuff tears: a randomized controlled trial.
Journal: The American Journal of Sports Medicine
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim benefit based on (+) secondary endpoint
abstract:
BACKGROUND: It is unclear whether leukocyte-poor (LP) or leukocyte-rich (LR) varieties of platelet-rich plasma (PRP) as an adjuvant to arthroscopic rotator cuff repair (ARCR) result in improved tendon healing rates.

PURPOSE: To perform a network meta-analysis of the randomized controlled trials in the literature to ascertain whether there is evidence to support the use of LP- or LR-PRP as an adjunct to ARCR.

METHODS: The literature search was based on the PRISMA (Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses) guidelines. Randomized controlled trials comparing LP- or LR-PRP with a control alongside ARCR were included. Clinical outcomes, including retears and functional outcomes, were compared using a frequentist approach to network meta-analysis, with statistical analysis performed using R. The treatment options were ranked using the P-score.

RESULTS: There were 13 studies (868 patients) included, with 9 studies comparing LP-PRP with a control and 4 studies comparing LR-PRP with a control. LP-PRP was found to significantly reduce the rate of retear and/or incomplete tendon healing after fixation, even among medium-large tears; it also improved outcomes on the visual analog scale for pain, Constant score, and University of California Los Angeles score. LP-PRP had the highest P-score for all treatment groups. LR-PRP did not result in any significant improvements over the control group, except for visual analog scale score for pain. However, post hoc analysis revealed that LP-PRP did not lead to significant improvements over LR-PRP in any category.

CONCLUSION: The current study demonstrates that LP-PRP reduces the rate of retear and/or incomplete tendon healing after ARCR and improves patient-reported outcomes as compared with a control. However, it is still unclear whether LP-PRP improves the tendon healing rate when compared with LR-PRP.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine you're fixing a broken toy.  Doctors are trying to figure out the best way to help a torn rotator cuff (a muscle in your shoulder) heal.  They looked at studies of different ways to help the healing, using a special blood fluid called platelet-rich plasma (PRP).  There are two types: one with lots of white blood cells (leukocyte-rich) and one with fewer white blood cells (leukocyte-poor).

The studies looked at which type of PRP, if any, helped the torn rotator cuff heal better.  They looked at how many times the tear happened again, how much pain the patients felt, and how well they could use their shoulder.

The results showed that the leukocyte-poor PRP helped a lot!  It made the tear happen less often and helped the shoulder feel better.  The leukocyte-rich PRP didn't seem to help much compared to no PRP at all.  But, the study didn't find that the leukocyte-poor PRP was *better* than the leukocyte-rich PRP.  So, it's still a bit of a mystery which is best.

Title: Analysis of Gastric Physiology After Laparoscopic Sleeve Gastrectomy (LSG) With or Without Antral Preservation in Relation to Metabolic Response: a Randomised Study.

abstract:
BACKGROUND: Immunometabolism is the interaction between immune system and nutrient metabolism. Severe obesity is considered a state of meta-inflammation associated with obesity that influences the development of chronic-degenerative diseases.OBJECTIVE: We aimed to establish the immunometabolic differences in bariatric patients and to determine whether cellular immunity is associated with metabolic changes.METHODOLOGY: We conducted an observational study in patients who underwent laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy (LSG) or laparoscopic Roux-en-Y gastric bypass (LRYGB). We explored the differences in the immunometabolic profile before and after surgery in the study group, by surgical technique, and we evaluated the changes in immunological variables as a function of metabolic variables with correlation analysis.

RESULTS: The follow-up rate was 88.7%. After the intervention, there were changes in cellular immunity, with a decrease in effector T lymphocytes (CD8+CD28-) and an increase in B lymphocytes, memory helper T cells, and cytotoxic T lymphocytes. LSG resulted in a greater decrease in (CD4+CD62-)вЂ‰T lymphocytes compared with LRYGB. Patients who underwent surgery with LRYGB presented greater clinical and metabolic improvements, as well as improvement of obesity-associated medical problems. Women who underwent LRYGB showed a greater reduction in fat-free mass compared with women who underwent LSG.

CONCLUSION: Bariatric surgery, mainly LRYGB, leads to immunometabolic changes and improves associated medical problems.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your body has a team of soldiers (the immune system) that fight off germs and keep you healthy.  Your body also needs fuel (nutrients) to work properly.  This study looked at how these two things – the immune system and how your body uses food – work together in people who are very overweight.

Specifically, they looked at how the immune system changed after weight-loss surgery (like gastric bypass).  They found that the immune system soldiers changed in number and type after surgery.  Some types of soldiers went down, while others went up.  The type of surgery mattered too; one type of surgery seemed to change the immune system more than the other.

The study also found that the surgery helped people's health problems related to being overweight.  People who had the more effective surgery (gastric bypass) saw bigger improvements in their health and how their bodies used food.  Interestingly, women who had the more effective surgery lost more of the non-fat part of their body.

In short, weight-loss surgery changes how your body's immune system and how it uses food, and this can help people who are very overweight feel better.

plain_language_summary:
Imagine your body has a team of soldiers (the immune system) that fight off germs and keep you healthy.  This team needs fuel (nutrients) to do its job.  The way these two things work together is called "immunometabolism."

This study looked at how the immune system and nutrient use change in people who are very overweight and had surgery to lose weight.  They did two types of surgery: one called a sleeve gastrectomy and the other a Roux-en-Y gastric bypass.

The study found that after surgery, the immune system soldiers changed.  Some types of soldiers went down, and others went up.  The type of surgery seemed to affect how much some of the soldiers changed.  The surgery that was better at helping people lose weight also seemed to change the immune system soldiers more.

Importantly, the study found that one type of surgery wasn't better than the other at helping people lose weight overall.  It also looked at how much muscle and fat people had, and found some differences between the two types of surgery in how much muscle was lost.
